title: In Search of the Hidden M-Bombs Treasure
text:
1.2 Overview
This book provides a comprehensive treatment of mathematical methods essential for understanding stochastic processes, diffusion phenomena, and their applications. The material is organized in four main parts:

Mathematics: Foundational topics including Gaussian integrals, laws of large numbers, Laplace method, and ordinary differential equations
Diffusion: Detailed treatment of diffusion processes from both mesoscopic and microscopic perspectives

Stochastic Processes: Advanced topics covering stochastic processes, Wiener processes, stochastic differential equations, Fokker-Planck equations, and their applications in physics
Advanced Topics: Research-level material including Turing patterns and additional applications

Each chapter includes worked examples, exercises, and practical applications in physics and applied mathematics.

For inquiries and feedback: dario.liotta.1@studenti.unipd.it

title: 2  Gaussian integrals
text: 3 Gaussian integrals

title: 2  Gaussian integrals
text:
3.1 The simplest form
Let’s consider the Gaussian function
\[\begin{equation}
    p(x)=ce^{-\frac{a}{2}x^2},   a\in\mathbb{R}^+
\end{equation}\]
If \(p(x)\) is a pdf (probability density function) then it must necessarily be normalized over the entire interval \(\mathbb{R}\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}p(x)dx=1 \ \Longrightarrow \ c\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}e^{-\frac{a}{2}x^2}dx=1
\end{equation*}\]
from which the value of \(c\) is obtained.
The integral has as its solution
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}e^{-\frac{a}{2}x^2}dx=\sqrt{\frac{2\pi}{a}}}
\end{equation}\]
where \(a\) - let’s remember - must necessarily be real and positive.

title: 3  The Laws of Large Numbers
text:
4.1 Joint Distribution
Let’s imagine we have two random variables \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) that are independent and also identically distributed, meaning they follow the same distribution law.
The joint distribution of these two variables, being independent, can be factored into the product of the individual distributions:
\[\begin{equation*}
    p\left(x_1,x_2\right)=p_1\left(x_1\right)p_2\left(x_2\right)
\end{equation*}\]
Specifically, being identically distributed, we can write:
\[\begin{equation*}
    p_1(x)=p_2(x)=q(x) \ \Longrightarrow \ p\left(x_1,x_2\right)=q\left(x_1\right)q\left(x_2\right)
\end{equation*}\]

title: 3  The Laws of Large Numbers
section: 4.3 The Weak Law of Large Numbers
text:
4.3 The Weak Law of Large Numbers
We now have all the tools we need to deal with the law of large numbers; actually, we will initially give a proof of a weaker formulation of it, and then state the stronger one.
The law is useful for establishing the behavior of the empirical mean of \(n\) independent and identically distributed variables as \(n\) itself increases, that is, of the values extracted from the distribution that is the same for all. In mathematical terms, we are interested in calculating:
\[\begin{equation}
    \lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nx_i
\end{equation}\]
We will prove that, if \(\mu\) is the mean of the distribution of each random variable, the result of the limit coincides precisely with \(\mu\). In reality, we will not prove the direct convergence of the limit to \(\mu\) but the so-called convergence in distribution, which is obtained through the characteristic function of the pdf: in this sense the law is interpreted as weak.
Let’s therefore write the characteristic function associated with the empirical mean \(x\):
\[\begin{align}
    \begin{split}
        \varphi(k)&=\left\langle e^{ikx}\right\rangle=\left\langle e^{i\frac{k}{n}\sum\limits_{i=1}^nx_i}\right\rangle=\int q\left(x_1\right)\dots q\left(x_n\right)e^{i\frac{k}{n}\sum\limits_{i=1}^nx_i}dx_1\dots dx_n=\\
        &=\int q\left(x_1\right)e^{i\frac{k}{n}x_1}dx_1\dotsc \int q\left(x_n\right)e^{i\frac{k}{n}x_n}dx_n=\varphi_i^n\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)
    \end{split}
\end{align}\]
where the index \(i\) indicates a generic value from \(1\) to \(n\), as all characteristic functions are equal since the variables are identically distributed.
Let’s make \(\varphi_i\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)\) explicit and expand the exponential in a Taylor series:
\[\begin{align*}
    \varphi_i\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)&=\int q\left(x_i\right)e^{i\frac{k}{n}x_i}dx_i=\int q\left(x_i\right)\left[1+i\frac{k}{n}x_i+\mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1}{n^2}\right)\right]dx_i=\\
    &=\int q\left(x_i\right)dx_i+i\frac{k}{n}\int x_iq\left(x_i\right)dx_i+\mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1}{n^2}\right)=1+i\frac{k}{n}\left\langle x_i\right\rangle+\mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1}{n^2}\right)
\end{align*}\]
But having assumed \(\left\langle x_i\right\rangle=\mu\) then
\[\begin{equation*}
    \varphi_i\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)=1+i\frac{k}{n}\mu+\mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1}{n^2}\right)
\end{equation*}\]
Returning to (eq:legge-dei-grandi-numeri-debole-funzione-caratteristica):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \varphi(k)=\varphi_i^n\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)=\left[1+i\frac{k}{n}\mu+\mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1}{n^2}\right)\right]^n
\end{equation*}\]
Finally, passing to the limit \(n\to\infty\) we are able to state that
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\varphi(k)=\left\langle e^{i\frac{k}{n}\sum\limits_{i=1}^nx_i}\right\rangle\to e^{ik\mu}}
\end{equation}\]
This means that, at the limit, the empirical mean is not in turn a random variable, but is a fixed number equal to \(\mu\). Mathematically we can therefore state that the distribution function of \(x\) at the limit is nothing more than a delta function, so much so that we can write:
\[\begin{equation*}
    e^{ik\mu}=\int\delta(x-\mu)e^{ikx}dx
\end{equation*}\]
Intuitively, we can imagine a decrease in statistical fluctuations around the mean \(\mu\) as the number of extractions of variables from the distribution increases.

title: 4  The Laplace method
text:
5 The Laplace method
In many situations it is necessary to calculate complicated integrals whose form is
\[\begin{equation}{}
    I(\lambda)=\int_{x_1}^{x_2}g(x)e^{\lambda f(x)}dx
\end{equation}\]
where \(f(x)\) and \(g(x)\) are real and smooth functions and \(\lambda\) is a real parameter.
Although it is impossible to calculate \(I(\lambda)\) with a fixed \(\lambda\), under appropriate conditions it is possible to approximate it well in the limit for \(\lambda\to\infty\): the idea of the Laplace method, in fact, is that the major contribution to the integral as \(\lambda\) increases comes from the neighborhood of the point where \(f(x)\) assumes its maximum value.

title: 5  Ordinary differential equation systems
text:
6.1 Review of dynamical systems
Many applications (physics, biology, chemistry, economics, …) lead to nonlinear systems of ordinary differential equations (ODEs) \[\begin{equation}
\begin{cases}
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t)=\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t)),\\[4pt]
\mathbf{x}(0)=\mathbf{x}_0,
\end{cases}
\end{equation}\] where \(\mathbf{x}(t)=(x_1(t),\dots,x_N(t))\in U\subseteq\mathbb{R}^N\), with \(U\) an open connected set. The map \(\mathbf{f}:U\to\mathbb{R}^N\) is called a and \(U\) is the . A solution \(\mathbf{x}(t;\mathbf{x}_0)\) is a trajectory; trajectories are smooth curves in phase space parametrised by time.
At each point \(\mathbf{x}\in U\) the vector \(\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})\) indicates the instantaneous direction of motion. By ``flowing’’ along the vector field a point traces a trajectory.
Definition (trajectory). A curve \(t\mapsto\mathbf{x}(t)\) satisfying \(\eqref{eq:autonomous}\) for \(t\) in some interval is a trajectory (or orbit) of the system.
Remark. For autonomous systems the vector field does not depend explicitly on time; time-translations of trajectories are again trajectories.

6.1.1 Nullclines and phase portraits
Nullclines are subsets of phase space where a component of the vector field vanishes. For a two-dimensional system \[
\begin{cases}
\dot{x}_1=f_1(x_1,x_2),\\[3pt]
\dot{x}_2=f_2(x_1,x_2),
\end{cases}
\] the \(x_1\)-nullcline is \(\{(x_1,x_2)\mid f_1(x_1,x_2)=0\}\) and the \(x_2\)-nullcline is \(\{f_2=0\}\). Intersections of nullclines are fixed points (equilibria). A phase portrait is the collection of trajectories with arrows showing the direction of motion.

title: 5  Ordinary differential equation systems
text:
6.2 Simple examples
Example 1 (one dimension). \[
\dot{x}=\sin x.
\] The vector field is scalar; equilibria occur where \(\sin x=0\), i.e. \(x=k\pi\), \(k\in\mathbb{Z}\). The sign of \(\sin x\) determines motion between equilibria.
Example 2 (two dimensions). \[
\begin{cases}
\dot{x}=1,\\[2pt]
\dot{y}=x^2+y^2.
\end{cases}
\]

title: 5  Ordinary differential equation systems
text:
6.3 Existence and uniqueness: Picard–Lindelöf theorem
Theorem (Picard–Lindelöf / Picard). Suppose \(\mathbf{f}:U\subset\mathbb{R}^N\to\mathbb{R}^N\) is continuous on an open set \(U\) and is Lipschitz in \(\mathbf{x}\) on a neighbourhood of \(\mathbf{x}_0\) (for instance if \(\mathbf{f}\) and all first partial derivatives \(\partial f_i/\partial x_j\) are continuous). Then there exists \(\delta>0\) and a unique solution \(\mathbf{x}(t)\) of \(\eqref{eq:autonomous}\) defined for \(t\in(-\delta,\delta)\) with \(\mathbf{x}(0)=\mathbf{x}_0\).
Corollary. Under the hypotheses above trajectories do not intersect in phase space.
Remarks. - Lipschitz continuity is the key hypothesis for uniqueness. Continuity alone guarantees existence but not uniqueness. - Solutions may blow up in finite time (maximal interval of existence may be bounded).

title: 5  Ordinary differential equation systems
text:
6.4 Fixed points and linearization
Definition (fixed point / equilibrium). A point \(\mathbf{x}^*\in U\) is a fixed point if \[
\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^*)=\mathbf{0}.
\] To study local behaviour near a fixed point we set \(\mathbf{u}(t)=\mathbf{x}(t)-\mathbf{x}^*\) and Taylor-expand: \[
\dot{\mathbf{u}}=A\,\mathbf{u}+ \mathbf{R}(\mathbf{u}),
\] where the Jacobian matrix \[
A\;=\;\left.\left(\frac{\partial f_i}{\partial x_j}\right)\right|_{\mathbf{x}^*}
\] and the remainder satisfies \(\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{u})=o(\|\mathbf{u}\|)\) as \(\mathbf{u}\to\mathbf{0}\). The is the linear system \[
\dot{\mathbf{u}}=A\mathbf{u}.
\] Question: when does the linearization correctly describe the nonlinear system? The next section gives a sufficient condition.

title: 5  Ordinary differential equation systems
section: 6.5 Hyperbolic fixed points and the Hartman–Grobman theorem
text:
6.5 Hyperbolic fixed points and the Hartman–Grobman theorem
Definition. A fixed point \(\mathbf{x}^*\) is if no eigenvalue \(\lambda\) of the Jacobian \(A\) has zero real part, i.e. \(\operatorname{Re}\lambda\neq0\) for all eigenvalues.
Theorem (Hartman–Grobman). If \(\mathbf{x}^*\) is a hyperbolic fixed point of \(\eqref{eq:autonomous}\), then there exists a homeomorphism mapping a neighbourhood of \(\mathbf{x}^*\) onto a neighbourhood of the origin that conjugates the nonlinear flow to the linear flow generated by \(A\). In other words, the phase portrait near a hyperbolic fixed point is topologically equivalent to that of the linearization.
Consequence. For a hyperbolic equilibrium the linearized system determines stability type (node, saddle, focus, etc.).

6.5.1 Explicit classification of \(2\times2\) linear systems
Consider the linear system \(\dot{\mathbf{u}}=A\mathbf{u}\) with \[
A=\begin{pmatrix} a & b\\[2pt] c & d\end{pmatrix},\qquad
T=\operatorname{tr}A=a+d,\qquad D=\det A=ad-bc,
\] and discriminant \[
\Delta \;=\; T^2-4D.
\] The eigenvalues are \(\lambda_{1,2}=(T\pm\sqrt{\Delta})/2\). The local phase portrait type at the origin is determined by the signs of \(T\), \(D\) and the value of \(\Delta\) as follows.
Summary decision flow (use these tests in order):
Remarks and practical checks:
Phase-portrait gallery for \(2\times 2\) linear systems (saddle, stable / unstable node, stable / unstable spiral, center) — see e.g. Strogatz §5.2. ## When linearization fails: non-hyperbolic cases and examples
Linearization is inconclusive when eigenvalues lie on the imaginary axis. Two instructive one-dimensional examples illustrate non-uniqueness and finite-time blow-up.
Example (finite-time blow-up). \[
\dot{y}=y^2,\qquad y(0)=1.
\] Separation gives \(\int y^{-2}\,dy=t+c\), so \(-y^{-1}=t+c\) and \[
y(t)=\frac{1}{-t-c}=\frac{1}{1-t}
\] (using initial condition to get \(c=-1\)). This solution blows up at \(t=1\), so it exists only on the maximal interval \((-\infty,1)\).
Example (non-uniqueness). \[
\dot{y}=\sqrt{y},\qquad y(0)=0.
\] Separation yields \(2\sqrt{y}=t+C\). One solution is \(y(t)=\frac{t^2}{4}\) for \(t\ge0\). However \(y(t)\equiv0\) is also a solution, and more generally the family \[
y_T(t)=\begin{cases}
0, & 0\le t\le T,\\[4pt]
\dfrac{(t-T)^2}{4}, & t>T,
\end{cases}\qquad T\ge0,
\] provides infinitely many solutions with the same initial condition. Non-uniqueness arises because the vector field \(\sqrt{y}\) fails to be Lipschitz at \(y=0\).

title: 5  Ordinary differential equation systems
text:
6.6 Stable, unstable and center manifolds (brief)
For a hyperbolic fixed point, the linear system decomposes the phase space into stable and unstable eigenspaces (associated with eigenvalues of negative or positive real part). For the nonlinear system there exist invariant stable and unstable manifolds tangent to those eigenspaces at the fixed point. (The center manifold theory treats eigenvalues with zero real part and requires finer analysis.)

title: 5  Ordinary differential equation systems
text:
6.7 Examples and exercises
Worked exercise. Solve \(\dot{y}=y^2\) with \(y(0)=1\). (See the blow-up example above.) Determine maximal interval of existence.
Exercise 1. Find and classify all fixed points of \[
\begin{cases}
\dot{x}=-x+x^3,\\[3pt]
\dot{y}=-2y.
\end{cases}
\] Identify stability of each fixed point.
Exercise 2. Consider \[
\begin{cases}
\dot{x}=-y+ax(x^2+y^2),\\[3pt]
\dot{y}=x+ay(x^2+y^2).
\end{cases}
\] Linearization about the origin yields a center for all \(a\) (pure imaginary eigenvalues). Use polar coordinates \(x=r\cos\theta\), \(y=r\sin\theta\) to derive the radial equation and determine the stability of the origin depending on \(a\). (Hint: derive \(\dot r\) and \(\dot\theta\) and analyse the radial dynamics.)
Exercise 3. A particle satisfies \(\ddot{x}=x-x^3\). Convert to first-order system and find fixed points. Show that the energy \[
E=\frac{\dot{x}^2}{2}-\frac{x^2}{2}+\frac{x^4}{4}
\] is conserved and use level sets of \(E\) to sketch the phase portrait.
Exercise 4. Study the population system \[
\begin{cases}
\dot{x}=x(3-x-2y),\\[3pt]
\dot{y}=y(2-x-y),
\end{cases}\qquad x,y\ge0,
\] find fixed points, classify them, and sketch the phase portrait and basins of attraction. (Compare with included figure.)

title: 5  Ordinary differential equation systems
text:
6.8 Remarks and further reading
This chapter collects basic notions used throughout the course: existence and uniqueness, equilibria, linear stability, and local equivalence (Hartman–Grobman). For more advanced topics see texts on nonlinear dynamics: center manifold theory, normal forms, bifurcation theory and invariant manifolds.

title: 6  Diffusion
text:
7 Diffusion
To introduce the stochastic processes that we will see later, it is necessary to delve deeper into the phenomenon of diffusion, which we have already mentioned in the previous chapter. In particular, we will derive the diffusion equation in different contexts and through two independent methods: the one related to the mesoscopic scale, which describes observable phenomena such as Brownian motion, and the one related to the microscopic scale, which will lead directly to one of the pillars in the study of stochastic processes, the random walk.

title: 6  Diffusion
text:
7.1 Einstein’s derivation
Let’s consider a long, thin tube in the \(x\) direction filled with water, into which ink is injected at the position \(x=0\) at time \(t=0\).

Let’s focus on a generic region \(V(x,t)\) inside the tube, so that we can define the following quantity:
\[\begin{equation}
    w(x,t)=\frac{\text{Number of particles in} \ V(x,t)}{V(x,t)}
\end{equation}\]
This is therefore the particle density of ink at position \(x\) at time \(t\), assuming that \(V(x,t)\) is small enough to be identified by a single value of \(x\).
We can define the probability that a particle is in a certain region \(A\) as
\[\begin{equation}
    \int_Aw(x,t)dx
\end{equation}\]
For it to actually correspond to a probability, it must be normalized, therefore it is necessary that
\[\begin{equation*}
    \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}w(x,t)dx=1
\end{equation*}\]
We can also easily establish the initial condition knowing that at time \(t=0\) the entire ink is concentrated at \(x=0\):
\[\begin{equation}
    w(x,0)=\delta(x)
\end{equation}\]
Our goal is to find a law that tells us how \(w(x,t)\) varies over time. We will therefore have to derive a formula that can express \(w(x,t+\Delta t)\) as a function of a quantity dependent on \(t\), and to do this we will need some important assumptions that we will see below.
Let’s consider the density \(w(x-y,t)\) associated with all the particles located at a distance \(y\) from the coordinate \(x\) at time \(t\). The first necessary assumption is the following:
 The probability that a particle moves a distance \(y\) in a time interval \(\Delta t\) is independent of the starting point considered
Let’s denote by \(f(y,\Delta t)\) the pdf relative to this probability, which as we have said depends only on \(y\) and not also on \(x\). In this case, the density \(w(x,t+\Delta t)\) can easily be expressed as
\[\begin{equation}
    w(x,t+\Delta t)=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}w(x-y,t)f(y,\Delta t)dy
\end{equation}\]
Intuitively we can say that the density will be obtained from the contribution of all possible values of \(y\), each of which is expressed by the product of the density of the particles at \(x-y\) multiplied by the probability \(f(y,\Delta t)dy\) that they move by \(y\).
To solve the integral equation we make a second assumption:
 The function \(f(y,\Delta t)\) decays quickly as \(y\) increases
This means that the major contributions come from the regions near \(x\), since at greater distances the probability of a displacement occurring becomes very low. This justifies us in expanding \(w(x-y,t)\) in a Taylor series with respect to \(x\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    w(x-y,t)=w(x,t)-\frac{\partial w(x,t)}{\partial x}y+\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2w(x,t)}{\partial x^2}y^2+\mathcal{O}\left(y^3\right)
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s substitute on the right-hand side of (eq:diffusione-derivazione-einstein-equazione-integrale), temporarily ignoring the higher-order terms:
\[\begin{equation*}
    w(x,t)\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(y,\Delta t)dy-\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}yf(y,\Delta t)dy+\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2w}{\partial x^2}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}y^2f(y,\Delta t)dy
\end{equation*}\]
The first integral is clearly equal to \(1\) since \(f(y,\Delta t)dy\) is a probability. As for the other two integrals, however, two other assumptions come to our aid; the first is the following:
 Displacements with respect to a coordinate \(x\) are equally probable from the left and from the right
Translated into a formula:
\[\begin{equation*}
    f(-y,\Delta t)=f(y,\Delta t)
\end{equation*}\]
Since it is a symmetric function in \(y\), therefore, the integral with the linear term in \(y\) will clearly be zero.
As for the last one, instead, the fourth and final assumption is the following:
 The second moment of the distribution \(f(y,\Delta t)\) is directly proportional to \(\Delta t\)
Let \(D\) be the proportionality constant, it follows that
\[\begin{equation}
    \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}y^2f(y,\Delta t)dy=D\Delta t
\end{equation}\]
Putting everything together we get
\[\begin{equation*}
    w(x,t+\Delta t)=w(x,t)+\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2w}{\partial x^2}D\Delta t+\mathcal{O}\left(y^3\right)
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s isolate the incremental ratio with respect to time on the left-hand side:
\[\begin{equation}
    \frac{w(x,t+\Delta t)-w(x,t)}{\Delta t}=\frac{D}{2}\frac{\partial^2w}{\partial x^2}+\mathcal{O}\left(y^3\right)
\end{equation}\]
At this point, going to the limit \(\Delta t\to0\) we obtain the diffusion equation, also called the heat equation because it describes the analogous thermodynamic phenomenon:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\frac{\partial w}{\partial t}=\frac{D}{2}\frac{\partial^2w}{\partial x^2}}
\end{equation}\]
This is a partial differential equation (PDE), which can be solved by imposing the initial condition seen in equation (eq:diffusione-derivazione-einstein-condizione-iniziale).
All the reasoning done can be easily extended to a greater number of spatial dimensions, obtaining the formula
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\frac{\partial w}{\partial t}=\frac{D}{2}\nabla^2w}
\end{equation}\]

7.1.1 Solution with dilation symmetry
Finding the solution of a PDE, in general, is not simple; to facilitate the calculations it is possible to exploit a symmetry of the system, called dilation symmetry, for which the solution to (eq:diffusione-derivazione-einstein-equazione-generale) is such as to preserve the ratio \(\frac{x}{\sqrt{t}}\).
To prove this, let’s suppose we go from the pair of coordinates \((x,t)\) to the pair of coordinates \(\left(\lambda x,\lambda^2t\right)\) and substitute in (eq:diffusione-derivazione-einstein-equazione-1D):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{\partial}{\partial t}w\left(\lambda x,\lambda^2t\right)=\frac{D}{2}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}w\left(\lambda x,\lambda^2t\right)
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s use the chain rule on both the left and the right:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{\partial}{\partial t}=\frac{\partial\left(\lambda^2t\right)}{\partial t}\frac{\partial}{\partial\left(\lambda^2t\right)}=\lambda^2\frac{\partial}{\partial\left(\lambda^2t\right)}   \frac{\partial}{\partial x}=\frac{\partial(\lambda x)}{\partial x}\frac{\partial }{\partial(\lambda x)}=\lambda\frac{\partial}{\partial(\lambda x)}
\end{equation*}\]
Substituting:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \cancel{\lambda^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial\left(\lambda^2 t\right)}w\left(\lambda x,\lambda^2t\right)=\frac{D}{2}\cancel{\lambda^2}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial (\lambda x)^2}w\left(\lambda x,\lambda^2t\right)
\end{equation*}\]
which is the same form as the starting one. This means that both \(w(x,t)\) and \(w\left(\lambda x,\lambda^2t\right)\) are solutions of the differential equation, and therefore the density function is such as to leave the ratio \(\frac{x^2}{t}\) unchanged since
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{x'^2}{t'}=\frac{\cancel{\lambda^2}x^2}{\cancel{\lambda^2}t}=\frac{x^2}{t}
\end{equation*}\]
or, analogously, the ratio \(\frac{x}{\sqrt{t}}\), as written above.
This invariance constrains the functional form of \(w(x,t)\) to the dependence on the parameter \(\frac{x}{\sqrt{t}}\), which can be considered as a single variable:
\[\begin{equation*}
    w(x,t)\equiv v\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{t}}\right)
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s therefore rewrite the derivatives by setting \(z=\frac{x}{\sqrt{t}}\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{\partial}{\partial t}w\equiv\frac{\partial}{\partial t}v=\frac{dv}{dz}\frac{\partial z}{\partial t}=-\frac{x}{2\sqrt{t^3}}\frac{dv}{dz}
\end{equation*}\]
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}w\equiv\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}v=\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial x}v\right)=\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{dv}{dz}\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}\right)=\frac{d^2v}{dz^2}\left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}\right)^2+\frac{dv}{dz}\cancel{\frac{\partial^2z}{\partial x^2}}=\frac{1}{t}\frac{d^2v}{dz^2}
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s substitute in the differential equation:
\[\begin{equation*}
    -\frac{x}{2\sqrt{t^3}}\frac{dv}{dz}=\frac{D}{2t}\frac{d^2v}{dz^2} \ \Longrightarrow \ \ -\frac{x}{\sqrt{t}}\frac{dv}{dz}=D\frac{d^2v}{dz^2}
\end{equation*}\]
Remembering that \(\frac{x}{\sqrt{t}}=z\):
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\frac{d^2v}{dz^2}+\frac{z}{D}\frac{dv}{dz}=0}
\end{equation}\]
This is an ordinary differential equation (ODE), which is much simpler to solve. We just need to set \(\rho=\frac{dv}{dz}\) to obtain a first-order differential equation:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{d\rho}{dz}+\frac{z}{D}\rho=0 \ \Longrightarrow \ \ \int\frac{d\rho}{\rho}=-\frac{1}{D}\int zdz \ \Longrightarrow \ \ \log\rho=-\frac{z^2}{2D}+c \ \Longrightarrow \ \rho=ae^{-\frac{z^2}{2D}}
\end{equation*}\]
being \(a=e^c\).
Returning to \(v\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{dv}{dz}=ae^{-\frac{z^2}{2D}} \ \Longrightarrow \ \ v(z)=a\int_0^ze^{-\frac{z'^2}{2D}}dz'+b
\end{equation*}\]
But knowing that \(v(z)\equiv w(x,t)\):
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{w(x,t)=\int_0^{\frac{x}{\sqrt{t}}}e^{-\frac{z^2}{2D}}dz+b}
\end{equation}\]
The integral is not solvable, so there is no real analytical form of the solution, but in any case it is a valid solution for the equation (eq:diffusione-derivazione-einstein-equazione-1D).
We can still calculate specific values, such as the initial density for \(t\to0\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \lim_{t\to0}w(x,t)=\lim_{t\to0}\int_0^{\frac{x}{\sqrt{t}}}e^{-\frac{z^2}{2D}}dz+b=\int_0^\infty e^{-\frac{z^2}{2D}}dz+b=a\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2D}}+b
\end{equation*}\]
At \(x=0\), instead, the density is equal to
\[\begin{equation*}
    w(0,t)=\cancel{\int_0^0e^{-\frac{z^2}{2D}}dz}+b=b
\end{equation*}\]
This solution, however, is not what is called fundamental, in the sense that it is not the one that allows all possible configurations: just note that the initial condition \(w(x,0)=\delta\left(x-x_0\right)\) is not possible with any combination of \(a\) and \(b\), since \(a\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2D}}+b\) is a constant quantity unlike the Dirac delta. Later we will derive the fundamental solution, but first we need to explore the microscopic point of view.

title: 6  Diffusion
6  Diffusion
text:
7.3 The continuous limit
Having reached this point, it is not yet completely clear why the random walk should be linked to the diffusion process described at the beginning of the chapter.
The means that allows us to pass from one scale (the microscopic one) to the other (the mesoscopic one) is the continuous limit, in which the quantities \(\Delta\ell\) and \(\Delta t\) are progressively reduced until they become infinitesimal.
We will therefore have to rewrite equation (eq:diffusion-master-equation) by making these quantities explicit; we will use the following conventions:
\[\begin{align*}
    x_i\equiv x \ \Longrightarrow \ \ &x_{i\pm1}=x_i\pm\Delta\ell\equiv x\pm\Delta\ell\\
    t_i\equiv t \ \Longrightarrow \ \ &t_{i\pm1}=t_i\pm\Delta t\equiv t\pm\Delta t
\end{align*}\]
We can therefore write:
\[\begin{equation}
    W(x,t+\Delta t)=W(x-\Delta\ell,t)p_++W(x+\Delta\ell,t)p_-
\end{equation}\]
At this point we can expand the functions on the right-hand side in series, assuming that \(x\gg\Delta\ell\) so that we can stop the expansion at the first order while still ensuring a good approximation:
\[\begin{align*}
    &W(x-\Delta\ell,t)=W(x,t)-\Delta\ell\frac{\partial W}{\partial x}+\frac{\Delta\ell^2}{2}\frac{\partial^2W}{\partial x^2}+\mathcal{O}\left(\Delta\ell^3\right)\\
    &W(x+\Delta\ell,t)=W(x,t)+\Delta\ell\frac{\partial W}{\partial x}+\frac{\Delta\ell^2}{2}\frac{\partial^2W}{\partial x^2}+\mathcal{O}\left(\Delta\ell^3\right)
\end{align*}\]
Let’s sum the two terms, remembering that the first is multiplied by \(p_+\) and the second by \(p_-\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \left(p_++p_-\right)W(x,t)-\left(p_+-p_-\right)\Delta\ell\frac{\partial W}{\partial x}+\left(p_++p_-\right)\frac{\Delta\ell^2}{2}\frac{\partial^2W}{\partial x^2}+\mathcal{O}\left(\Delta\ell^3\right)
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s substitute in (eq:diffusione-limite-continuo-1) remembering that \(p_++p_-=1\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    W(x,t+\Delta t)=W(x,t)-\left(p_+-p_-\right)\Delta\ell\frac{\partial W}{\partial x}+\frac{\Delta\ell^2}{2}\frac{\partial^2W}{\partial x^2}+\mathcal{O}\left(\Delta\ell^3\right)
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s bring \(W(x,t)\) to the left-hand side and divide by \(\Delta t\):
\[\begin{equation}
    \frac{W(x,t+\Delta t)-W(x,t)}{\Delta t}=-\left(p_+-p_-\right)\frac{\Delta\ell}{\Delta t}\frac{\partial W}{\partial x}+\frac{\Delta\ell^2}{2\Delta t}\frac{\partial^2W}{\partial x^2}+\mathcal{O}\left(\Delta\ell^3\right)
\end{equation}\]
It is clear at this point that, going to the limit for \(\Delta t\to0\), the left-hand side would be reduced to the derivative of \(W(x,t)\) with respect to time, provided that \(t\gg\Delta t\); at the same time, as said before, we will also have to send \(\Delta\ell\to0\). We note that on the right we have two ratios dependent on \(\Delta\ell\) and \(\Delta t\), which are not defined when we pass to the limit.
Let’s therefore define the following quantities:
\[\begin{equation}
    \begin{cases}
        \left(p_+-p_-\right)\lim\limits_{\Delta\ell,\Delta t\to0}\frac{\Delta\ell}{\Delta t}=v\\
        \lim\limits_{\Delta\ell,\Delta t\to0}\frac{\Delta\ell^2}{\Delta t}=D
    \end{cases}
\end{equation}\]
In other words, we are stating that the ratio between \(\Delta\ell\) and \(\Delta t\), as well as that between \(\Delta\ell^2\) and the same \(\Delta t\), is kept constant, so that physical meaning is guaranteed to the model.
The equation in its final form will therefore be
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\frac{\partial W}{\partial t}=-v\frac{\partial W}{\partial x}+\frac{D}{2}\frac{\partial^2W}{\partial x^2}}
\end{equation}\]
The quantity \(v\) has been so named precisely because it has the dimensions of a velocity, and represents the displacement of the particle to the left or to the right: we note in fact that for \(p_+=p_-=\frac{1}{2}\) the velocity is zero, since there is no tendency in one direction with respect to the other; in the case where \(p_+\neq p_-\) there will be a drift to the right if \(p_+>p_-\) or to the left if \(p_+<p_-\).
In this way we have rediscovered equation (eq:diffusione-derivazione-einstein-equazione-generale), generalizing to the case where \(v\neq0\).

7.3.1 Fundamental solution of the diffusion equation
Having found the most general expression for the diffusion equation, we now want to calculate the fundamental solution, that is, the one that is able to solve almost any diffusion problem starting from a generic initial condition. Since we are going to solve a partial differential equation, a condition that must necessarily be respected is the boundedness of the solution: this would not be necessary in a finite interval, but since it is formally defined in all of \(\mathbb{R}\) then it is necessary that it also be bounded, since otherwise it would not be a unique solution.
In particular, we will impose that
\[\begin{equation*}
    w(x,0)=\delta\left(x-x_0\right)
\end{equation*}\]
Although the Dirac delta \(\delta\left(x-x_0\right)\) is a function that diverges at \(x=x_0\), in fact, what is important is that its integral over all of \(\mathbb{R}\) is finite: in this way the evolution over time of the distribution can spread but certainly not condense to the point of diverging at some point, since the area underneath is still finite.
Let’s now proceed to the solution, for which it is necessary to resort to the Fourier transform with respect to the spatial coordinate:
\[\begin{equation}
    \tilde{w}(\omega,t)=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}w(x,t)e^{i\omega x}dx
\end{equation}\]
First we will see the simplest case in which \(v=0\), while later we will also calculate the solution in the more general case with \(v\neq0\).
Let’s apply the transform to the first member:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \mathcal{F}\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial t}\right)=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{\partial w}{\partial t}e^{i\omega x}dx=\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}w(x,t)e^{i\omega x}dx=\frac{\partial\tilde{\omega}}{\partial t}
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s now apply it to the second member:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{D}{2}\mathcal{F}\left(\frac{\partial^2w}{\partial x^2}\right)=\frac{D}{2}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{\partial^2w}{\partial x^2}e^{i\omega x}dx
\end{equation*}\]
To solve this integral we must integrate by parts twice:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{\partial^2w}{\partial x^2}e^{i\omega x}dx=\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}e^{i\omega x}\bigg|_{-\infty}^{+\infty}-\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\frac{\partial e^{i\omega x}}{\partial x}dx
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s remember that \(w(x,t)\) is nothing more than a distribution, so it is legitimate to assume that for \(x\to\pm\infty\) it (and its two successive derivatives) tend to zero. In this way the first term will be zero and we can repeat the same reasoning with the second term:
\[\begin{align*}
    \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{\partial^2w}{\partial x^2}e^{i\omega x}dx&=-i\omega\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}e^{i\omega x}dx=\\
    &=-i\omega\left[\cancel{w(x,t)e^{i\omega x}\big|_{-\infty}^{+\infty}}-\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}w(x,t)\frac{\partial e^{i\omega x}}{\partial x}dx\right]=\\
    &=-i\omega\left[-i\omega\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}w(x,t)e^{i\omega x}dx\right]=-\omega^2\tilde{w}(\omega,t)
\end{align*}\]
We thus obtain the following first-order differential equation:
\[\begin{equation}
    \frac{\partial\tilde{\omega}}{\partial t}=-\frac{\omega^2D}{2}\tilde{w}(\omega,t)
\end{equation}\]
It is solvable by separation of variables:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{d\tilde{w}}{dt}=-\frac{\omega^2D}{2}\tilde{w} \ \Longrightarrow \ \ \int\frac{d\tilde{w}}{\tilde{w}}=-\frac{\omega^2D}{2}\int dt \ \Longrightarrow \ \ \log\tilde{w}=-\frac{\omega^2D}{2}t+c
\end{equation*}\]
The solution, therefore, will be
\[\begin{equation}
    \tilde{w}(\omega,t)=ke^{-\frac{\omega^2D}{2}t}
\end{equation}\]
By imposing \(w(x,0)=\delta\left(x-x_0\right)\) we can apply the initial condition to obtain the unique solution:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \tilde{w}(\omega,0)=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}w(x,0)e^{i\omega x}dx=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\delta\left(x-x_0\right)e^{i\omega x}dx=e^{i\omega x_0}
\end{equation*}\]
Ultimately, it results:
\[\begin{equation}
    \tilde{w}(\omega,t)=e^{i\omega x_0-\frac{\omega^2D}{2}t}
\end{equation}\]
To return to the form \(w(x,t)\) we will have to resort to the inverse transform:
\[\begin{equation}
    w(x,t)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\tilde{w}(\omega,t)e^{-i\omega x}d\omega=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}e^{-\frac{\omega^2D}{2}t-i\omega\left(x-x_0\right)}d\omega
\end{equation}\]
This integral is none other than a Gaussian integral in the most general form seen in equation (eq:integrali-gaussiani-forma-generale), in which \(a=Dt\) and \(b=-i\left(x-x_0\right)\), from which the solution follows:
\[\begin{equation}
    w(x,t)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2\pi}{Dt}}e^{-\frac{\left(x-x_0\right)^2}{2Dt}} \ \Longrightarrow \ \boxed{w(x,t)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi Dt}}e^{-\frac{\left(x-x_0 \right)^2}{2Dt}}}
\end{equation}\]
The most important thing to note is that \(w(x,t)\) is normalized over the entire spatial interval at any instant of time, in fact:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}w(x,t)dx=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi Dt}}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}e^{-\frac{\left(x-x_0 \right)^2}{2Dt}}dx=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi Dt}}\sqrt{2\pi Dt}=1
\end{equation*}\]
This means that we can interpret \(w(x,t)\) as a probability density function, and this will be useful in the calculations that we will see below.
So far we have assumed for simplicity that the diffusion process started from the instant \(t=0\). To extend the reasoning to a generic initial instant \(t_0\) we will have to rewrite the initial condition as
\[\begin{equation*}
    w\left(x,t_0\right)=\delta\left(x-x_0\right)
\end{equation*}\]
The procedure, on the whole, does not change; the only real difference is from (eq:diffusione-limite-continuo-eq-diff-trasformata), from which the constant \(k\) will take the value
\[\begin{equation*}
    \tilde{w}\left(\omega,t_0\right)=e^{i\omega x_0} \ \Longrightarrow \ \ e^{i\omega x_0}=ke^{-\frac{\omega^2D}{2}t_0} \ \iff \ \ k=e^{i\omega x_0+\frac{\omega^2D}{2}t_0}
\end{equation*}\]
We thus find:
\[\begin{equation}
    \tilde{w}(\omega,t)=e^{i\omega x_0-\frac{\omega^2D}{2}\left(t-t_0\right)}
\end{equation}\]
At this point the inverse transform will not change going from \(t\) to \(t-t_0\), so the final solution will be
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{w(x,t)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi D\left(t-t_0\right)}}e^{-\frac{\left(x-x_0\right)^2}{2D\left(t-t_0\right)}}}
\end{equation}\]
This relationship demonstrates the translational invariance of the solution with respect to both space and time. The meaning is that this quantity depends only on the difference between the spatial coordinates \(x-x_0\) and the temporal ones \(t-t_0\), and there are no privileged positions or privileged instants of time with respect to others; mathematically, in fact, we can state that
\[\begin{equation}
    w\left(x,t|x_0,t_0
\right)=w\left(x-x_0,t-t_0|0,0
\right)
\end{equation}\]
If we assume that \(v\neq0\) then, when we apply the Fourier transform, on the right-hand side of the diffusion equation we will also have to consider the contribution
\[\begin{equation*}
    -v\mathcal{F}\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)
\end{equation*}\]
From the calculations seen above we know that
\[\begin{equation*}
    \mathcal{F}\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)=-i\omega\mathcal{F}(w)
\end{equation*}\]
from which it follows:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{\partial\tilde{\omega}}{\partial t}=-\left(\frac{\omega^2D}{2}-i\omega v\right)\tilde{w}(\omega,t)
\end{equation*}\]
From (eq:diffusione-estensione-a-tempo-t_0) we can easily understand how the solution of this differential equation is
\[\begin{equation*}
    \tilde{w}(\omega,t)=e^{i\omega x_0-\left(\frac{\omega^2D}{2}-i\omega v\right)\left(t-t_0 \right)}
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s now perform the inverse transform:
\[\begin{equation*}
    w(x,t)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\tilde{w}(\omega,t)e^{-i\omega x}d\omega=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}e^{-\frac{\omega^2D}{2}\left(t-t_0 \right)-i\omega\left[x-x_0-v\left(t-t_0 \right)\right]}d\omega
\end{equation*}\]
The Gaussian integral will give:
\[\begin{equation}
\boxed{w(x,t)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi D\left(t-t_0
\right)}}e^{-\frac{\left[x-x_0-v\left(t-t_0
\right)\right]^2}{2D\left(t-t_0
\right)}}}
\end{equation}\]
The results obtained can be generalized to a generic number of dimensions greater than one. Interpreting \(w\left(\vec{x},t\right)\) as the probability distribution associated with the particles in a system of (hyper)volume \(V\) and (hyper)surface \(\Sigma(V)\), and indicating with \(\vec{J}(\vec{x},t)\) the flux density of the particles themselves, from the continuity equation it results:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{d}{dt}\int_Vw\left(\vec{x},t\right)d^nx=-\int_{\Sigma(V)}\vec{J}\left(\vec{x},t\right)\cdot\hat{n}dS
\end{equation*}\]
where \(\hat{n}\) is the unit vector orthogonal to the surface unit \(dS\).
From the divergence theorem, moreover, we can also reduce to a volume integral for \(\vec{J}\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \int_{\Sigma(V)}\vec{J}\left(\vec{x},t\right)\cdot\hat{n}dS=\int_V\vec{\nabla}\cdot\vec{J}\left(\vec{x},t\right)d^nx
\end{equation*}\]
Substituting in the continuity equation and bringing everything to the left-hand side inside a single integral:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \int_V\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial t}+\vec{\nabla}\cdot\vec{J}\right)d^nx=0
\end{equation*}\]
From this it follows that
\[\begin{equation}
    \frac{\partial w}{\partial t}+\vec{\nabla}\cdot\vec{J}=0 \ \Longrightarrow \ \frac{\partial w}{\partial t}=-\vec{\nabla}\cdot\vec{J}
\end{equation}\]
Since there are no external fields, we can assume that in a first approximation the flux density is proportional to the opposite of the gradient of the particle density, since they tend to diffuse from regions of high density to those of low density. For reasons of dimension, the proportionality coefficient is the diffusion coefficient \(D\):
\[\begin{equation}
    \vec{J}\left(\vec{x},t\right)\simeq-D\vec{\nabla}w\left(\vec{x},t\right)
\end{equation}\]
This approximation is known as Fick’s law, which substituted in (eq:diffusione-dimensioni-maggiori-1) returns
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{\partial w}{\partial t}\simeq\vec{\nabla}\left[D\vec{\nabla}w\left(\vec{x},t\right)\right]
\end{equation*}\]
Assuming \(D\) is constant, finally:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\frac{\partial w}{\partial t}\simeq D\nabla^2w\left(\vec{x},t\right)}
\end{equation}\]
We thus rediscover the formula (eq:diffusione-derivazione-einstein-equazione-generale-dim>1) that we had already anticipated, except for a factor of \(\frac{1}{2}\) due to a simple approximation of Fick’s law.


7.3.2 Solutions via propagator
The peculiarity of the fundamental solution written in (eq:diffusione-soluzione-fondamentale-generale) is that it describes how the initial information from a point \(x_0\) in time; for this reason it is also called the propagator of the diffusion process.
As reported previously, the fundamental solution is so called because from it all the solutions of the diffusion equation can be derived starting from any initial condition, provided - as said - that the boundedness of the function is guaranteed. In other words, we can say that all solutions can be written starting from the propagator of the relative equation.
It is not difficult to verify that the function
\[\begin{equation}
    W(x,t)=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(y)w\left(x,t|y,t_0\right)dy
\end{equation}\]
is a solution of the problem
\[\begin{equation}
    \begin{cases}
        \frac{\partial W}{\partial t}=\frac{D}{2}\frac{\partial^2W}{\partial x^2}\\
        W\left(x,t_0\right)=f(x)
    \end{cases}
\end{equation}\]
assuming that \(f\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{R})\).
To prove this, let’s calculate the time derivative of \(W(x,t)\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{\partial W}{\partial t}=\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(y)w\left(x,t|y,t_0\right)dy=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(y)\frac{\partial w\left(x,t|y,t_0\right)}{\partial t}dy
\end{equation*}\]
From the diffusion equation (with zero drift velocity):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{\partial w}{\partial t}=\frac{D}{2}\frac{\partial^2w}{\partial x^2} \ \Longrightarrow \ \frac{\partial W}{\partial t}=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(y)\frac{D}{2}\frac{\partial^2w\left(x,t|y,t_0\right)}{\partial x^2}dy
\end{equation*}\]
From which we find:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{\partial W}{\partial t}=\frac{D}{2}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(y)w\left(x,t|y,t_0\right)dy=\frac{D}{2}\frac{\partial^2W}{\partial x^2}
\end{equation*}\]
which is precisely the equation of the system (eq:diffusione-limite-continuo-problema-generale).
By making \(W\left(x,t_0\right)\) explicit, moreover, we have
\[\begin{equation*}
    W\left(x,t_0\right)=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(y)w\left(x,t_0|y,t_0\right)dy=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(y)\delta(x-y)dy=f(x)
\end{equation*}\]
which is the expected initial condition.
The reasoning can be generalized for \(v\neq0\), in which case (eq:diffusione-limite-continuo-soluzione-generale) is a solution of the system
\[\begin{equation}
    \begin{cases}
        \frac{\partial W}{\partial t}=-v\frac{\partial W}{\partial x}+\frac{D}{2}\frac{\partial^2W}{\partial x^2}\\
        W\left(x,t_0\right)=f(x)
    \end{cases}
\end{equation}\]


7.3.3 Generation of moments via propagator
As anticipated, the normalization of the propagator with respect to the whole space, which implies its role as a pdf, is useful for the calculation of quantities such as the average position or the variance with respect to the coordinates \(x\), or more generally for the generation of moments.
We can in fact write:
\[\begin{equation}
    \langle f(x)\rangle_t=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)w\left(x,t|x_0,t_0\right)dx
\end{equation}\]
To easily calculate the moments up to the second order, we can calculate the derivative with respect to time:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{d}{dt}\langle f(x)\rangle_t=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)\frac{\partial w}{\partial t}dx=-v\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}dx+\frac{D}{2}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)\frac{\partial^2w}{\partial x^2}dx
\end{equation*}\]
Remembering that \(w\left(x,t|x_0,t_0\right)\) converges to zero at infinity, both on one side and on the other, we can integrate the first integral by parts and repeat the same thing twice for the second, exactly as done during the calculations that led to equation (eq:diffusione-limite-continuo-equazione-differenziale-primo-ordine-trasformata). We will obtain:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\frac{d}{dt}\langle f(x)\rangle_t=v\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{df}{dx}w\left(x,t|x_0,t_0\right)dx+\frac{D}{2}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}w\left(x,t|x_0,t_0\right)dx}
\end{equation}\]
We can calculate the average position of the distribution by setting \(f(x)=x\):
\[\begin{equation}
    \frac{d}{dt}\langle x\rangle_t=v\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}w\left(x,t|x_0,t_0\right)dx+0=v \ \Longrightarrow \ \boxed{\langle x\rangle_t=x_0+v\left(t-t_0\right)}
\end{equation}\]
To calculate the variance we must first calculate the second-order moment:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{d}{dt}\left\langle x^2\right\rangle_t=v\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}2xw\left(x,t|x_0,t_0\right)dx+\frac{D}{2}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}2w\left(x,t|x_0,t_0\right)dx=2v\langle x\rangle_t+D
\end{equation*}\]
Substituting \(\langle x\rangle_t\) from (eq:diffusione-media-posizione):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{d}{dt}\left\langle x^2\right\rangle_t=2vx_0+2v^2\left(t-t_0 \right)+D
\end{equation*}\]
Integrating:
\[\begin{equation}
    \left\langle x^2\right\rangle_t=x_0^2+2vx_0\left(t-t_0
\right)+v^2\left(t-t_0
\right)^2+D(t-t_0)
\end{equation}\]
At this point we can calculate the variance:
\[\begin{align*}
    &Var(x)=\left\langle x^2\right\rangle_t-\langle x\rangle_t=\\
    &=\cancel{x_0^2}\cancel{+2vx_0\left(t-t_0
\right)}\cancel{+v^2\left(t-t_0
\right)^2}+D(t-t_0)\cancel{-x_0^2}\cancel{-2vx_0\left(t-t_0
\right)}\cancel{-v^2\left(t-t_0
\right)^2}
\end{align*}\]
What remains is therefore
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{Var(x)=D\left(t-t_0 \right)}
\end{equation}\]
Both results were actually predictable: looking at the expression (eq:diffusione-soluzione-fondamentale-generale) we notice that it is a Gaussian centered at \(x_0+v\left(t-t\right)\) with standard deviation \(\sqrt{D\left(t-t_0\right)}\). It is, in practice, a Gaussian initially centered at \(x_0\), which tends to move over time with constant velocity \(v\), and whose initial null variance (being a Dirac delta) then tends to increase proportionally to \(D\), thus spreading the distribution throughout space.


7.3.4 Summary table

title: 6  Diffusion
text:
7.4 Diffusion in a semi-infinite domain
So far we have considered diffusion problems that involved all of infinite space. For the last part of this chapter we will also explore two distinct cases of semi-infinite spaces, that is, spaces bounded by a wall beyond which unlimited space extends.
We will see how delimiting the space in this way implies the addition of a new constraint to be respected for the propagator of the diffusion process. The calculations will be done assuming the simplest case in which \(v=0\) and \(t_0=0\), as the results are then easily generalizable to the case with \(v\neq0\) and \(t_0\neq0\).

7.4.1 Absorbing wall
Let’s assume as a first case that the wall has absorbing properties, that is, that every particle that reaches this wall is absorbed by it and therefore removed from the system.
For simplicity of calculation, let’s assume that this wall is located at \(x=0\) and that the possible space for the diffusion of particles is \(\mathbb{R}^+\). The new constraint to be imposed, therefore, is the following:
\[\begin{equation*}
    w(0,t)=0
\end{equation*}\]
which translated indicates that at any instant of time there are no particles at the coordinate \(x=0\), as they are absorbed and removed from the system.
Indicating with \(w_a(x,t)\) the propagator relative to this problem, the system to be solved is therefore
\[\begin{equation}
    \begin{cases}
        \frac{\partial w_a}{\partial t}=\frac{D}{2}\frac{\partial^2w_a}{\partial x^2}\\
        w_a(x,0)=\delta\left(x-x_0\right)\\
        w_a(0,t)=0
    \end{cases}
\end{equation}\]
The goal, therefore, is to find a solution that is fixed at zero at the origin during the entire evolution and that at the same time satisfies the conditions already seen previously. One idea is to take two distributions that are symmetric with respect to the origin, that evolve in the same way, and subtract them from each other. If we want the distribution to be centered at \(x_0\), for example, we will have to consider the one centered at \(-x_0\), thus obtaining
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{w_a\left(x,t|x_0,0
\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi Dt}}\left(e^{-\frac{\left(x-x_0
\right)^2}{2Dt}}-e^{-\frac{\left(x+x_0
\right)^2}{2Dt}}\right)}
\end{equation}\]
We can prove that this is a valid solution of the system (eq:diffusione-parete-assorbente-sistema). In the first place, both distributions are solutions of the differential equation; moreover, since the equation is linear, a linear combination of solutions is in turn a solution of the same equation, and (eq:diffusione-parete-assorbente-propagatore) is precisely a linear combination of two valid solutions.
As for the initial condition, it will be given by the difference of the Dirac deltas \(\delta\left(x-x_0\right)-\delta\left(x+x_0\right)\). This is not a problem, as the space considered is only that of the positive coordinates, so one of the two deltas will not contribute:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \int_0^{+\infty}w\left(x,0|x_0,0
\right)dx=\int_0^{+\infty}\delta\left(x-x_0
\right)dx-\cancel{\int_0^{+\infty}\delta\left(x+x_0
\right)dx}=1
\end{equation*}\]
This guarantees the normalization of the distribution at the initial instant of time, so even if the initial condition of the system (eq:diffusione-parete-assorbente-sistema) is not formally respected, it is still a valid solution for it.
Having found the propagator of the system, the general solution with initial condition \(f(x)\) will therefore be given by
\[\begin{equation}
    W_a(x,t)=\int_0^{+\infty}f(y)w_a\left(x,t|y,0\right)dy
\end{equation}\]
The main difference of the propagator found with respect to the propagator in infinite space is related to its normalization: \(w_a(x,t)\), in fact, is normalized only at the instant \(t=0\), while for \(t>0\) its integral gives as a result a number less than \(1\).
To observe this we must make this integral explicit, which we indicate with \(P(t)\) as it is dependent on time:
\[\begin{align*}
    P(t)&=\int_0^{+\infty}w_a\left(x,t|x_0,0\right)dx=\\
    &=\int_0^{+\infty}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi Dt}}e^{-\frac{\left(x-x_0
\right)^2}{2Dt}}dx-\int_0^{+\infty}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi Dt}}e^{-\frac{\left(x+x_0
\right)^2}{2Dt}}dx
\end{align*}\]
Let’s perform a change of variable for both integrals:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \begin{cases}
        u=\frac{x-x_0}{\sqrt{Dt}}\\
        v=\frac{x+x_0}{\sqrt{Dt}}
    \end{cases}
    \Longrightarrow \
    \begin{cases}
        du=\frac{dx}{\sqrt{Dt}}\\
        dv=\frac{dx}{\sqrt{Dt}}
    \end{cases}
\end{equation*}\] \end{equation*}
\[\begin{equation*}
    P(t)=\int_{-\frac{x_0}{\sqrt{Dt}}}^{+\infty}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{u^2}{2}}du-\int_{\frac{x_0}{\sqrt{Dt}}}^{+\infty}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{v^2}{2}}dv
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s rewrite the first integral as the difference between the integral over the whole space and the complementary interval:
\[\begin{equation*}
    P(t)=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{u^2}{2}}du-\int_{-\infty}^{-\frac{x_0}{\sqrt{Dt}}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{u^2}{2}}du-\int_{\frac{x_0}{\sqrt{Dt}}}^{+\infty}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{v^2}{2}}dv
\end{equation*}\]
The first integral is a normalized Gaussian integral; in the second integral, since the integrand function is symmetric, we can the integration interval, obtaining:
\[\begin{equation*}
    P(t)=1-\int_{\frac{x_0}{\sqrt{Dt}}}^{+\infty}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{v^2}{2}}dv-\int_{\frac{x_0}{\sqrt{Dt}}}^{+\infty}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{v^2}{2}}dv=1-2\int_{\frac{x_0}{\sqrt{Dt}}}^{+\infty}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{v^2}{2}}dv
\end{equation*}\]
Since the resulting integral is positive for \(t>0\), we have therefore shown that
\[\begin{equation*}
    P(t>1)<1
\end{equation*}\]
just as stated initially. This quantity is also called survival probability as it expresses the probability that a particle has not been absorbed at time \(t\).
From \(P(t)\) we can also calculate the lifetime distribution, indicated with \(q(t)\), which corresponds to the probability distribution associated with the absorption of a particle. In particular, the decrease of \(P(t)\) over time indicates an increase in particles that are absorbed, therefore
\[\begin{equation*}
    q(t)=-\frac{dP}{dt}
\end{equation*}\]
The problem at this point is that calculating the derivative of \(P(t)\) is not trivial, because the Gaussian integral is not solvable in the interval \(\left[\frac{x_0}{\sqrt{Dt}},+\infty\right)\). This forces us to look for another way to calculate the derivative of the survival probability.
The idea is to make the probability \(P\) explicit according to the definition and exploit the diffusion equation associated with the propagator:
\[\begin{align*}
    \frac{dP}{dt}&=\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_0^{+\infty}w_a\left(x,t|x_0,0\right)dx=\int_0^{+\infty}\frac{\partial w_a}{\partial t}dx=\frac{D}{2}\int_0^{+\infty}\frac{\partial^2w_a}{\partial x^2}dx=\\
    &=\frac{D}{2}\frac{\partial w_a}{\partial x}\bigg|_0^{+\infty}=0-\frac{D}{2}\frac{\partial w_a}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0}=\\
    &=-\frac{D}{2}\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi Dt}}\left(e^{-\frac{\left(x-x_0
\right)^2}{2Dt}}-e^{-\frac{\left(x+x_0
\right)^2}{2Dt}}\right)\right]\bigg|_{x=0}=\\
    &=-\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{D}{2\pi t}}\left(-\frac{x-x_0}{Dt}e^{-\frac{\left(x-x_0
\right)^2}{2Dt}}\bigg|_{x=0}+\frac{x+x_0}{Dt}e^{-\frac{\left(x+x_0
\right)^2}{2Dt}}\bigg|_{x=0}\right)=\\
    &=-\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{D}{2\pi t}}\frac{x_0}{Dt}\left(e^{-\frac{x_0^2}{2Dt}}+e^{-\frac{x_0^2}{2Dt}}\right)=-\frac{1}{\cancel{2}}\frac{x_0}{\sqrt{2\pi Dt^3}}\cdot\cancel{2}e^{-\frac{x_0^2}{2Dt}}
\end{align*}\]
From which it follows:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{q(t)=-\frac{dP}{dt}=\frac{x_0}{\sqrt{2\pi Dt^3}}e^{-\frac{x_0^2}{2Dt}}}
\end{equation}\]
In particular, we find that
\[\begin{equation}
    q(t)\sim t^{-\frac{3}{2}}
\end{equation}\]
This trend is characteristic of the diffusion process we are studying, namely Brownian motion.


7.4.2 Reflecting wall
Let’s now imagine that the wall at \(x=0\) is reflecting, that is, capable of sending back every particle that happens to hit it. To do this we must impose that the flow of particles at \(x=0\) is zero, in the sense that every particle that reaches the wall at the instant \(t\), not being able to cross it, no longer has any to the left, but on the contrary is to the right, and because of this symmetry the net flow is zero:
\[\begin{equation*}
    J(x=0,t)=0
\end{equation*}\]
From Fick’s law (equation (eq:diffusione-legge-di-fick)) we know that the flow is given by
\[\begin{equation*}
    J(x,t)=-\frac{D}{2}\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}
\end{equation*}\]
Therefore the condition to be imposed will be:
\[\begin{equation}
    \frac{\partial w_r}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0}=0
\end{equation}\]
At this point we proceed as in the absorbing case: we exploit the symmetry of the system and consider a linear combination of the two distributions centered one at \(x_0\) and the other at \(-x_0\).
\[\begin{equation*}
    w_r\left(x,t|x_0,0\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi Dt}}\left(\alpha e^{-\frac{\left(x-x_0
\right)^2}{2Dt}}+\beta e^{-\frac{\left(x+x_0
\right)^2}{2Dt}}\right)
\end{equation*}\]
The goal is to find the coefficients \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\). We therefore impose the condition (eq:diffusione-parete-riflettente-condizione):
\[\begin{align*}
    \frac{\partial w_r}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0}&=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi Dt}}\left(-\alpha\frac{x-x_0}{Dt}e^{-\frac{\left(x-x_0
\right)^2}{2Dt}}-\beta\frac{x+x_0}{Dt}e^{-\frac{\left(x+x_0
\right)^2}{2Dt}}\right)\bigg|_{x=0}=\\
    &=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi Dt}}\left(\frac{\alpha x_0}{Dt}e^{-\frac{x_0^2}{2Dt}}-\frac{\beta x_0}{Dt}e^{-\frac{x_0^2}{2Dt}}\right)=\frac{x_0}{\sqrt{2\pi D^3t^3}}(\alpha-\beta)e^{-\frac{x_0^2}{2Dt}}
\end{align*}\]
This implies that
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{\partial w_r}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0}=0 \ \iff \ \alpha=\beta
\end{equation*}\]
By setting \(\alpha=\beta=1\) the solution will therefore be given by
\[\begin{equation*}
    \boxed{w_r\left(x,t|x_0,0\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi Dt}}\left(e^{-\frac{\left(x-x_0
\right)^2}{2Dt}}+e^{-\frac{\left(x+x_0
\right)^2}{2Dt}}\right)}
\end{equation*}\]
Again, having found the propagator, we can write the general solution:
\[\begin{equation}
    W_r(x,t)=\int_0^{+\infty}f(y)w_r\left(x,t|x_0,0\right)dy
\end{equation}\]

title: 7  Stochastic Processes
text:
8 Stochastic Processes
We have already mentioned Brownian motion, characterized by a set of particles moving randomly, each independent of the others. Like any physical phenomenon, Brownian motion is also described by a mathematical model, in this case called a stochastic model due to its intrinsic random nature: this is the Wiener process, which we will explore in more detail below.
More generally, we will delve into the study of multiple models, described by stochastic differential equations whose solutions are continuous trajectories. Such models are used in many fields of physics and beyond (biology, finance, and so on). We will limit ourselves to exploring them to the point where we can use the tools in practical applications, so the treatment as a whole will be non-rigorous from a mathematical point of view, ignoring the concepts and rigors of measure theory, which is the formal mathematical basis in the field of stochastic processes.

title: 7  Stochastic Processes
text:
8.1 Introduction to Probability Theory
Before talking about stochasticity, it is necessary to introduce the theory that underlies it: probability theory. Below we will see how to define the fundamental unit of stochastic processes, namely random variables.
Let’s start with a simple example, typical in the introduction of statistics courses: the coin toss. If we toss a coin once, two outcomes are possible: heads or tails; if we toss it twice, the number of outcomes doubles, from two to four, and so on; by tossing a coin \(N\) times, therefore, \(2^N\) total outcomes will be possible, also called realizations of the random experiment. The set of realizations is called the sample space and is denoted by \(\Omega\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \Omega=\left\{(0,0,\dots,0),(1,0,\dots,0),(0,1,\dots,0),(1,1,\dots,0),\dots,(1,1,\dots,1)\right\}
\end{equation*}\]
having indicated, by convention, the outcome “heads” with \(1\) and the outcome “tails” with \(0\).
Let’s now imagine we want to take a measurement related to this experiment, for example, the number of heads. The number of possible measurements will clearly be \(N+1\), and these define what is called the state space \(E\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    E=\{0,1,2,\dots,N\}
\end{equation*}\]
A random variable \(Y\), from a mathematical point of view, is nothing more than a function that maps elements from the sample space to the state space:
\[\begin{equation}
    Y:\Omega\to E
\end{equation}\]
Furthermore, this function must be . This occurs if for any subset \(E_S\subseteq E\) the pre-image of the function relative to \(Y^{-1}\left(E_S\right)\), which would be
\[\begin{equation*}
    Y^{-1}\left(E_S\right)=\left\{\omega\in\Omega|Y(\omega)\in E_S\right\}
\end{equation*}\]
is a measurable set in \(\Omega\). As already mentioned in the premises, we will not delve too deeply into the formal aspects, but it is enough for us to know that without this condition we could not define the concept of probability.
To each element \(y_i\in E\), in fact, we can associate the probability \(p_i\) through the following formula:
\[\begin{equation}
    p_i=\sum_{\omega\in\Omega:Y(\omega)=y_i}P(\omega)
\end{equation}\]
where \(P(\omega)\) is the probability relative to the realization \(\omega\). Returning to the coin toss, for example, each realization has the same probability of occurring as the others, so it will be \(P\left(\omega_1\right)=P\left(\omega_2\right)=\dotsc=P\left(\omega_{2^N}\right)\). Formally we define the function \(P\) as
\[\begin{equation*}
    P:\Omega\to[0,1]:\sum_{\omega\in\Omega}P(\omega)=1
\end{equation*}\]
Due to the normalization to one, if all realizations have the same probability, then we will assign to each of them \(P\left(\omega_i\right)=\frac{1}{2^N}\).
If the set \(\Omega\) is finite, then \(Y\) is certainly measurable, and it is therefore always possible to define a random variable. The problem becomes more complicated when we consider uncountable sets, because in that case it makes no sense to assign a probability to a single element of \(\Omega\). We will in fact have to associate probabilities with intervals, that is, with sets called events, which can be bounded only from above or also from below.
Indicating the random variable with \(X\), the possibilities are as follows:
\[\begin{align*}
    &\{X\leqslant x\}=\{\omega\in\Omega|X(\omega)\leqslant x\\
    &\{a\leqslant X\leqslant b\}=\{\omega\in\Omega|a\leqslant X(\omega)\leqslant b
\end{align*}\]
where \(x,a,b\in\mathbb{R}\).

title: 7  Stochastic Processes
text:
8.2 Definition of a Stochastic Process
We now have the tools to define what a stochastic process is.

A **stochastic process** is a *collection* of random variables

\begin{equation*}
    \{x(t,\omega),t\in T,\omega\in\Omega\}
\end{equation*}

where $T$ is an **ordered set** and $\Omega$ is a **sample space**.

Given a fixed time \(\bar{t}\), the quantity \(x\left(\bar{t},\omega\right)\) indicates a random variable that maps elements of \(\Omega\) into the state space \(E\) relative to the process.
By fixing \(\omega=\bar{\omega}\), instead, we obtain a function of time \(x\left(t,\bar{\omega}\right)\) that describes a deterministic trajectory, which is called a sample path or stochastic realization.
In general, the stochastic variable is indicated for simplicity by \(x(t)\), ignoring the dependence on \(\omega\), because what is measured is a single realization; moreover, in the context of stochastic processes, mainly the global properties are studied, such as mean, variance, and so on, which depend solely on time, being calculated with respect to all possible realizations in \(\Omega\).
Figure (fig:processi-stocastici-esempio-semplice) shows a simple example of a stochastic process in which \(T=\{0,1,2,3\}\) and \(E=\{-1,0,1\}\), with a particle that can move through the three states of \(E\) at the time instants indicated by the elements of \(T\). In particular, three different stochastic realizations are shown, associated therefore with different \(\omega\).

The random walk that we introduced in the last chapter is nothing more than a stochastic process in which \(T=\mathbb{N}\) and \(E=\mathbb{Z}\). We recall that in that case there is a constraint according to which the particle is forced to move from its position at each time step. Some realizations are shown in figure (fig:processi-stocastici-random-walk-esempio).



Realizations of a random walk


With the random walk, we move to an infinite, but still countable, number of trajectories. For the description of continuous systems, such as Brownian motion, we must move to a number of trajectories that is not only infinite, but also uncountable: in that case, in fact, we will have a process in which \(T=\mathbb{R}^+\) and \(E=\mathbb{R}\). A condition that is imposed, in this specific case, is that the entire distribution of particles is centered at the origin, as we can observe in the example reported in figure (fig:processi-stocastici-processo-browniano-esempio).



Realizations of a Brownian process


In this last example, each realization is nothing more than a function \(x(t)\in C^0([0,T])\).

text:
10 Stochastic differential equations
We have already written a first stochastic differential equation in (eq:processi-stocastici-prima-equazione-differenziale-stocastica) when talking about the simulation of Brownian motion through a computer. The purpose now is to deepen this type of equation, trying to give a mathematical formalism that is not only linked to the simulation processes obtained through finite difference approximations.

title: 9  Stochastic differential equations
9  Stochastic differential equations
text:
10.2 Langevin equation
Let’s try to generalize what has been written so far. Let’s start from an ordinary differential equation, which we know has the form
\[\begin{equation*}
    dx=f(x,t)dt
\end{equation*}\]
To introduce the stochastic component we could proceed in the most general way, that is, by making the function also depend on the Brownian motion expressed by the function \(B\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    dx=f(x,t,B)dt
\end{equation*}\]
The problem with an equation of this type is that it is, in general, very difficult to approach. By introducing the noise in an additive form, we find an equation called the Langevin equation:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{dx=\mu(x,t)dt+\sigma dB}
\end{equation}\]
having rewritten the function \(f(x,t)\) as \(\mu(x,t)\) for consistency with the previous section, and being \(\sigma\) a constant coefficient.
Although the additive form is simpler to treat, it still hides aspects that are not identifiable through standard mathematical analysis. To understand in what sense, let’s consider the simple case in which \(\mu(x,t)\equiv-\mu x\): 
\[\begin{equation}
    dx=-\mu xdt+\sigma dB
\end{equation}\]
Let’s consider the average value on the first and second members:
\[\begin{equation*}
    d\cancel{\langle x\rangle}=-\mu\langle x\rangle dt+\sigma d\cancel{\langle B\rangle} \ \Longrightarrow \ \frac{d}{dt}\langle x\rangle=-\mu\langle x\rangle
\end{equation*}\]
This differential equation is known to give rise to the following solution:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \langle x(t)\rangle=x_0e^{-\mu t}
\end{equation*}\]
To calculate the variance, we will have to multiply both members of (eq:sde-equazione-di-langevin-caso-lineare) by \(2x\) and proceed in a similar way:
\[\begin{equation*}
    2xdx=-2\mu x^2dt+2\sigma xdB \ \Longrightarrow \ 2\langle xdx\rangle=-2\mu\left\langle x^2\right\rangle dt+2\sigma\langle xdB\rangle
\end{equation*}\]
The reason why we multiplied by \(2x\) is that the derivative of \(x^2\) is
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{d\left(x^2\right)}{dt}=\frac{d\left(x^2\right)}{dx}\frac{dx}{dt}=2x\frac{dx}{dt} \ \Longrightarrow \ d\left(x^2\right)=2xdx \ \Longrightarrow \ d\left\langle x^2\right\rangle=2\langle xdx\rangle
\end{equation*}\]
Returning to the previous expression, we will therefore have that
\[\begin{equation}
    d\left\langle x^2\right\rangle=-2\mu\left\langle x^2\right\rangle dt+2\sigma\langle xdB\rangle
\end{equation}\]
We can reasonably assume that the increments \(dB\) are uncorrelated with the coordinate \(x\), in the sense that they do not depend on it; it follows that:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \langle xdB\rangle=\langle x\rangle d\cancel{\langle B\rangle}=0 \ \Longrightarrow \ d\left\langle x^2\right\rangle=-2\mu\left\langle x^2\right\rangle dt \ \Longrightarrow \ \frac{d}{dt}\left\langle x^2\right\rangle=-2\mu\left\langle x^2\right\rangle
\end{equation*}\]
The solution of this differential equation is
\[\begin{equation*}
    \left\langle x(t)^2\right\rangle=x_0^2e^{-2\mu t}
\end{equation*}\]
A variance follows:
\[\begin{equation*}
    Var[x(t)]=\left\langle x^2\right\rangle-\langle x\rangle^2=x_0^2e^{-2\mu t}-\left(x_0e^{-\mu t}\right)^2=0
\end{equation*}\]
In essence, we would have shown that, despite the introduction of noise, the fluctuations are completely absent. This is obviously an absurdity, and depends on the fact that we cannot apply standard analysis for equations that contemplate stochastic terms such as those relating to the trajectories of Brownian motion.

title: 9  Stochastic differential equations
9  Stochastic differential equations
text:
10.4 Solution of the stochastic integral
The \(X_n\) defined in the theorem, in the limit, is none other than the stochastic integral written in equation (eq:sde-rumore-moltiplicativo-integrale-stocastico-primo-accenno). In essence, we have shown that
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\int_0^tB(t')dB(t')=\frac{B(t)^2}{2}+(\lambda-\frac{1}{2})t}
\end{equation}\]
The result of the integral therefore depends on the choice of \(\lambda\), which we remember is between zero and one. We will see below two possible choices, which involve different interpretations of the stochastic contribution.

10.4.1 Itô integral (\(\lambda=0\))
Following the Itô convention, let’s set \(\lambda=0\), obtaining:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\int_0^tB(t')dB(t')=\frac{B(t)^2}{2}-\frac{t}{2}}
\end{equation}\]
This choice is the most widespread because it brings several advantages with it, all due to an important property: by choosing \(\lambda=0\), in fact, the intermediate point \(\lambda t_i+(1-\lambda)t_{i-1}\) is equal to \(t_{i-1}\). This means that the integrand function is evaluated at the initial point of each interval of the partition: in other words, the integrand does not depend in any way on a time but only on past and present values. Functions of this type are called non-anticipative functions, and are the basis of mathematical analysis in the statistical field declined according to the Itô convention, called Itô calculus, which we will deepen later.
A first advantage of this convention is that the function \(\sigma(t)\) and the differential \(dB(t)\) are independent of each other, since the latter is relative to a future interval while the non-anticipative function depends exclusively on the initial point. In a non-rigorous way, we can therefore write:
\[\begin{equation}
    \left\langle\int_0^t\sigma(t')dB(t')\right\rangle=\int_0^t\langle\sigma(t')\rangle\langle dB(t')\rangle
\end{equation}\]
Since the differences of \(B(t)\) are distributed according to a Gaussian centered at zero, the average value of \(dB(t')\) will be zero, therefore
\[\begin{equation*}
    \left\langle\int_0^t\sigma(t')dB(t')\right\rangle=\int_0^t\langle\sigma(t')\rangle\cancel{\langle dB(t')\rangle}=0
\end{equation*}\]
This property is also confirmed by the particular case in which \(\sigma(t)=B(t)\), as is evident from equation (eq:sde-integrale-di-ito):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \left\langle\int_0^tB(t')dB(t')\right\rangle=\frac{\left\langle B(t)^2\right\rangle}{2}-\frac{t}{2}=\frac{t}{2}-\frac{t}{2}=0
\end{equation*}\]
In general, the Itô integral is linked to the discrete form by the following relation:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\int_0^t\sigma(t')dB(t')=\mslim_{n\to\infty}\sum_{i=1}^n\sigma\left(t_{i-1}\right)\left[B\left(t_i\right)-B\left(t_{i-1}\right)\right]}
\end{equation}\]
being \(\sigma(t)\) a non-anticipative function, and therefore depending exclusively on \(t_{i-1}\).
The link between Itô calculus and standard analysis is given by the following relation:
\[\begin{equation}
    \int_0^t\sigma(t')dB(t')^2=\int_0^t\sigma(t')dt'
\end{equation}\]
This, in fact, connects the two fields through the equality of the two differentials: on the one hand we have the infinitesimal square of Brownian motion, while on the other the differential of time. The first cannot exist in standard analysis, because, as we have repeated several times, the function \(B(t)\) is not differentiable; nevertheless, in integral calculus, the two differentials are actually equal, thus creating a point of connection between the two .
For the proof we must consider the following limit:
\[\begin{equation}
    \lim_{n\to\infty}\left\langle\left[\sum_{i=1}^n\sigma_{i-1}\left(\Delta B_i^2-\Delta t_i\right)\right]^2\right\rangle
\end{equation}\]
having written for simplicity \(\sigma_{i-1}=\sigma\left(t_{i-1}\right)\), \(\Delta B_i^2=\left[B\left(t_i\right)-B\left(t_{i-1}\right)\right]^2\) and \(\Delta t_i=t_i-t_{i-1}\).
Let’s expand the square, also considering the double products:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \lim_{n\to\infty}\left\langle\sum_{i=1}^n\sigma_{i-1}^2\left(\Delta B_i^2-\Delta t_i\right)^2+2\sum_{i>j}\sigma_{i-1}\sigma_{j-1}\left(\Delta B_i^2-\Delta t_i\right)\left(\Delta B_j^2-\Delta t_j\right)\right\rangle
\end{equation*}\]
The term \(\sigma_{i-1}\) is independent of the increments \(\Delta B_i\) due to the non-anticipative property of the Itô convention. We can therefore distribute the average value among all the individual terms:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \lim_{n\to\infty}\left[\sum_{i=1}^n\left\langle\sigma_{i-1}^2\right\rangle\left\langle\left(\Delta B_i^2-\Delta t_i\right)^2\right\rangle+2\sum_{i>j}\left\langle\sigma_{i-1}\sigma_{j-1}\right\rangle\left(\left\langle\Delta B_i^2\right\rangle-\Delta t_i\right)\left(\left\langle\Delta B_j^2\right\rangle-\Delta t_j\right)\right]
\end{equation*}\]
In the mixed terms, the contributions in parentheses are zero, since
\[\begin{equation*}
    \left\langle\Delta B_i^2\right\rangle-\Delta t_i=\Delta t_i-\Delta t_i=0
\end{equation*}\]
In the surviving term we can expand the square:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \left\langle\left(\Delta B_i^2-\Delta t_i\right)^2\right\rangle=\left\langle\Delta B_i^4\right\rangle-2\left\langle\Delta B_i^2\right\rangle\Delta t_i+\Delta t_i^2=3\Delta t_i^2-2\Delta t_i^2+\Delta t_i^2=2\Delta t_i^2
\end{equation*}\]
Substituting in the starting limit:
\[\begin{equation*}
    2\lim_{n\to\infty}\sum_{i=1}^n\left\langle\sigma_{i-1}^2\right\rangle\Delta t_i^2
\end{equation*}\]
By majorizing as we have already done previously as a function of \(\abs{P_n}\), we find that the limit converges to zero:
\[\begin{align*}
    2\lim_{n\to\infty}\sum_{i=1}^n\left\langle\sigma_{i-1}^2\right\rangle\Delta t_i^2&\leqslant2\lim_{n\to\infty}\left(\max_i\abs{t_i-t_{i-1}}\sum_{i=1}^n\left\langle\sigma_{i-1}^2\right\rangle\Delta t_i\right)=\\
    &=2\lim_{n\to\infty}\left(\abs{P_n}\sum_{i=1}^n\left\langle\sigma_{i-1}^2\right\rangle\Delta t_i\right)=0
\end{align*}\]
Returning to the limit of (eq:sde-ito-differenziali-uguali-limite-iniziale), setting \(x_n\) equal to the summation and \(x=0\), we can write
\[\begin{equation*}
    \lim_{n\to\infty}\left\langle\left(x_n-x\right)^2\right\rangle=0
\end{equation*}\]
This expression defines the mean squared limit for \(x_n\), that is, for the summation:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \mslim_{n\to\infty}\sum_{i=1}^n\sigma_{i-1}\left(\Delta B_i^2-\Delta t_i\right)=0
\end{equation*}\]
which can also be rewritten as
\[\begin{equation*}
    \mslim_{n\to\infty}\left(\sum_{i=1}^n\sigma_{i-1}\Delta B_i^2-\sum_{i=1}^n\sigma_{i-1}\Delta t_i\right)=0
\end{equation*}\]
Isolating the two terms:
\[\begin{equation}
    \mslim_{n\to\infty}\sum_{i=1}^n\sigma_{i-1}\Delta B_i^2=\mslim_{n\to\infty}\sum_{i=1}^n\sigma_{i-1}\Delta t_i
\end{equation}\]
According to the Itô convention, the two mean squared limits are none other than the integrals written in (eq:sde-ito-relazione-collante), thus proving the written relation.

Let \(g(t)\) be a non-anticipative stochastic process, that is, dependent on \(B(t')\) for every \(t'<t\) extit{.}
*Show that*


Solution. \[\begin{equation*}
        \left\langle\left(\int_0^tg(t')dB(t')\right)^2\right\rangle=\left\langle\int_0^tg(t')^2dt'\right\rangle
    \end{equation*}\]
*using the Itô convention.*

The proof follows similarly to the one just reported regarding $dB^2=dt$.

The expected value of the integral can be written as a function of its discretized form as follows:

\begin{equation*}
    \lim_{n\to\infty}\left\langle\left[\sum_{i=1}^ng_{i-1}\Delta B_i\right]^2\right\rangle=\lim_{n\to\infty}\left\langle\sum_{i=1}^ng_{i-1}^2\Delta B_i^2+2\sum_{i>j}g_{i-1}g_{j-1}\Delta B_i\Delta B_j\right\rangle
\end{equation*}

having decomposed the square, also contemplating the double products.

The non-anticipativity of $g(t)$ means that it is independent of any increment of $B(t')$ where $t'>t$, so we can distribute the expected value in the following way:

\begin{align*}
    &\lim_{n\to\infty}\left(\sum_{i=1}^n\left\langle g_{i-1}^2\right\rangle\left\langle \Delta B_i^2\right\rangle+2\sum_{i>j}\left\langle g_{i-1}g_{j-1}\right\rangle\cancel{\left\langle\Delta B_i\right\rangle}\cancel{\left\langle\Delta B_j\right\rangle}\right)=\\
    &=\lim_{n\to\infty}\sum_{i=1}^n\left\langle g_{i-1}^2\right\rangle\Delta t_i
\end{align*}

We thus find:

\begin{equation*}
    \lim_{n\to\infty}\sum_{i=1}^n\left\langle g_{i-1}^2\right\rangle\Delta t_i=\lim_{n\to\infty}\left\langle\sum_{i=1}^ng_{i-1}^2\Delta t_i\right\rangle=\left\langle\int_0^tg(t')^2dt'\right\rangle
\end{equation*}



10.4.2 Stratonovich integral (\(\lambda=\frac{1}{2}\))
Following the Stratonovich convention, let’s set \(\lambda=\frac{1}{2}\), obtaining:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\int_0^tB(t')dB(t')=\frac{B(t)^2}{2}}
\end{equation}\]
In this case the average value of the integral is not zero, since
\[\begin{equation*}
    \left\langle\int_0^tB(t')dB(t')\right\rangle=\frac{\left\langle B(t)^2\right\rangle}{2}=\frac{t}{2}
\end{equation*}\]
Intuitively, this means that the Brownian motion \(B(t)\) and its increment \(dB(t)\) are not uncorrelated with each other, and this depends on the fact that the point considered in which the function is evaluated at the limit of the Riemann sum is the intermediate point between \(t_{i-1}\) and \(t_i\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \lambda t_i+(1-\lambda)t_{i-1} \ \xrightarrow{\lambda=\frac{1}{2}} \ \frac{1}{2}t_i+\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right)t_{i-1}=\frac{1}{2}t_i+\frac{1}{2}t_{i-1}=\frac{1}{2}\left(t_i+t_{i-1}\right)
\end{equation*}\]
This means that intrinsically, in the calculation, we partially consider a time in each interval, which precisely constrains the correlation between the two quantities.
The Stratonovich convention is mostly used in the theoretical field, especially in the physical one, where the choice of the intermediate point corresponds to the most behavior in the evolution of a system in which dissipative effects and stochastic fluctuations intervene. To this is added the fact that in the choice of Stratonovich the chain rule of standard mathematical analysis is preserved, and this allows one to easily perform substitutions of variables as is common in calculations in physics.
In this sense, it is often said that choosing the Stratonovich convention means ; nevertheless, below we will exclusively deepen the Itô calculus, which finds more practical applications, especially in the field of finance, where all calculations are carried out taking into consideration only the knowledge at the current time, thus not having to consider future events.
