11 Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Process
To conclude the study of stochastic processes, which began with the analysis of diffusion processes, let’s take as an example a model that we will study with the various tools we have learned in the past chapters. The process in question is the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process, which, to be honest, we have already introduced in exercise (ex:processo-di-ornstein-uhlenbeck) following the introduction of the Wiener process.

title: 10  Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Process
10  Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Process
11  Fokker-Planck Equation
text:
12 Fokker-Planck Equation
Having defined and set up the stochastic differential equations, we are now interested in studying the statistics associated with the processes defined by these equations. To do this, and therefore to derive the various moments, we need to find the relative probability density functions.

title: 11  Fokker-Planck Equation
11  Fokker-Planck Equation
text:
12.2 Derivation for the Langevin equation
To obtain a general theoretical formula, we must first find an equation relative to \(p(x,t)\) which, when solved, will provide us with the pdf we need.
To obtain a formula, let’s consider a function \(f\) applied to the generic trajectory \(x(t)\), such that it satisfies two conditions:
In this way we can say that \(f(x(t))\) is twice differentiable, and also that for \(x\to\pm\infty\) both \(f(x(t))\) and its derivatives go to zero, since the support, as we have said, is limited.
To obtain a statistic on this function, let’s calculate the expected value, which by definition is given by
\[\begin{equation*}
    \langle f(x(t))\rangle=\lim_{N\to\infty}\sum_{i=1}^Nf\left(x^{(i)}(t)\right)
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s rewrite \(f\left(x^{(i)}(t)\right)\) as a function of the Dirac delta:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \langle f(x(t))\rangle=\lim_{N\to\infty}\sum_{i=1}^N\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)\delta\left(x-x^{(i)}(t)\right)dx
\end{equation*}\]
We note at this point that we can compose the terms of the second member until we obtain (eq:fokker-planck-densita-con-delta):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \langle f(x(t))\rangle=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)\lim_{N\to\infty}\sum_{i=1}^N\delta\left(x-x^{(i)}(t)\right)dx \ \Longrightarrow
\end{equation*}\]
\[\begin{equation*}
    \Longrightarrow \ \langle f(x(t))\rangle=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)p(x,t)dx
\end{equation*}\]
Differentiating on the left and on the right with respect to time:
\[\begin{equation}
    \frac{d}{dt}\langle f(x(t))\rangle=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)\frac{\partial p}{\partial t}dx
\end{equation}\]
The goal now is to find another expression for the derivative of the average value of \(f(x(t))\), this time as a function of \(p(x,t)\), so as to equate the two quantities and find a differential equation associated with the probability density.
We can expand the increment \(f(x+\Delta x)\) in a Taylor series:
\[\begin{equation*}
    f(x+\Delta x)=f(x)+\frac{df}{dx}\Delta x+\frac{1}{2}\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\Delta x^2+\mathcal{O}\left(\Delta x^3\right)
\end{equation*}\]
In temporal terms, \(x+\Delta x\) can be written as:
\[\begin{equation*}
    x(t)+\Delta x=x(t+\Delta t) \ \iff \ \Delta x=x(t+\Delta t)-x(t)
\end{equation*}\]
At this point we must substitute \(\Delta x\) starting from the stochastic differential equation we are considering. Let’s choose the most general Langevin equation, the one shown in (eq:sde-equazione-di-langevin-generalizzata), in which the multiplicative noise is included; it follows that
\[\begin{equation}
    \Delta x=\mu(x,t)\Delta t+\sigma(x,t)\Delta B
\end{equation}\]
being \(\Delta B=B(t+\Delta t)-B(t)\).
Returning to the series expansion, let’s bring \(f(x(t))\) to the first member and substitute the expression for \(\Delta x\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    f(x(t+\Delta t))-f(x(t))=\frac{df}{dx}(\mu\Delta t+\sigma\Delta B)+\frac{1}{2}\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}(\mu\Delta t+\sigma\Delta B)^2+\mathcal{O}\left(\Delta x^3\right)
\end{equation*}\]
By definition, the first member is none other than \(\Delta f\). On the right, instead, let’s expand the square, remembering to ignore the terms of order higher than \(\Delta t\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \Delta f=\frac{df}{dx}\mu\Delta t+\frac{df}{dx}\sigma\Delta B+\frac{1}{2}\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\sigma^2\Delta B^2+\mathcal{O}\left(\Delta x^3\right)
\end{equation*}\]
Remembering that \(\Delta B^2=\Delta t\), and applying the average value to both members, it results:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \langle\Delta f\rangle=\left\langle\frac{df}{dx}\mu\right\rangle\Delta t+\left\langle\frac{df}{dx}\sigma\Delta B\right\rangle+\frac{1}{2}\left\langle\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\sigma^2\right\rangle\Delta t+\mathcal{O}\left(\Delta x^3\right)
\end{equation*}\]
Thanks to the Itô condition:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \left\langle\frac{df}{dx}\sigma\Delta B\right\rangle=\left\langle\frac{df}{dx}\sigma\right\rangle\cancel{\langle\Delta B\rangle}=0
\end{equation*}\]
It follows that, by dividing both members by \(\Delta t\) and taking the limit for \(\Delta t\to0\), it results:
\[\begin{equation}
    \lim_{\Delta t\to0}\left\langle\frac{\Delta f}{\Delta t}\right\rangle=\left\langle\frac{df}{dx}\mu\right\rangle+\frac{1}{2}\left\langle\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\sigma^2\right\rangle
\end{equation}\]
We will have that
\[\begin{equation*}
    \lim_{\Delta t\to0}\left\langle\frac{\Delta f}{\Delta t}\right\rangle=\frac{d}{dt}\langle f(x(t))\rangle
\end{equation*}\]
This means that we can equate the second member of (eq:fokker-planck-derivazione-prima-uguaglianza) with the second member of (eq:fokker-planck-derivazione-seconda-uguaglianza):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)\frac{\partial p}{\partial t}dx=\left\langle\frac{df}{dx}\mu\right\rangle+\frac{1}{2}\left\langle\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\sigma^2\right\rangle
\end{equation*}\]
The second member, by definition, can be written as
\[\begin{equation*}
    \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)\frac{\partial p}{\partial t}dx=\left\langle\frac{df}{dx}\mu+\frac{1}{2}\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\sigma^2\right\rangle=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\left[\frac{df}{dx}\mu+\frac{1}{2}\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\sigma^2\right]p(x,t)dx
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s decompose the integral on the right into a sum of integrals:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)\frac{\partial p}{\partial t}dx=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{df}{dx}\mu(x,t)p(x,t)dx+\frac{1}{2}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\sigma(x,t)^2p(x,t)dx
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s integrate both integrals on the right-hand side by parts:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{df}{dx}\mu(x,t)p(x,t)dx=\cancel{f(x)\mu(x,t)p(x,t)\big|_{-\infty}^{+\infty}}-\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(\mu p)dx
\end{equation*}\]
\[\begin{align*}
    \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\sigma(x,t)^2p(x,t)dx&=\cancel{\frac{df}{dx}\sigma(x,t)^2p(x,t)\bigg|_{-\infty}^{+\infty}}-\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{df}{dx}\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\sigma^2p\right)dx=\\
    &=-\cancel{f(x)\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\sigma^2p\right)\bigg|_{-\infty}^{+\infty}}+\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}\left(\sigma^2p\right)dx
\end{align*}\]
Let’s substitute:
\[\begin{align*}
    \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)\frac{\partial p}{\partial t}dx&=-\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(\mu p)dx+\frac{1}{2}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}\left(\sigma^2p\right)dx=\\
    &=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}f(x)\left[-\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(\mu p)+\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}\left(\sigma^2p\right)\right]dx
\end{align*}\]
The equality follows:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\frac{\partial p}{\partial t}=-\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(\mu p)+\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}\left(\sigma^2p\right)}
\end{equation}\]
This is called the Fokker-Planck equation, also called the forward Fokker-Planck equation because it is opposed to the backward Kolmogorov equation, in which time instead flows backwards. Let’s remember that the equation just found is valid only for the Langevin equation and assuming the Itô condition is valid.

12.2.1 Conservation of probability
By setting
\[\begin{equation}
    J(x,t)=\mu(x,t)p(x,t)-\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left[\sigma(x,t)^2p(x,t)\right]
\end{equation}\]
we note that the Fokker-Planck equation takes on a well-known form:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{\partial}{\partial t}p(x,t)=-\frac{\partial}{\partial x}J(x,t)
\end{equation*}\]
which corresponds to the continuity equation, characteristic of the phenomena of diffusion of currents in which there is local conservation of fluxes.
Integrating in a domain \(\mathcal{D}\subseteq\mathbb{R}\), in fact:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{\mathcal{D}}p(x,t)dx=-\int_{\mathcal{D}}\frac{\partial J}{\partial x}dx=-J(x,t)\big|_{x\in\partial\mathcal{D}}
\end{equation*}\]
where \(\partial\mathcal{D}\) is the boundary of the domain \(\mathcal{D}\). For the probability to be conserved over time, therefore, it is necessary that the flux, expressed by \(J(x,t)\), be zero at the boundary at all instants of time.


12.2.2 Boundary conditions
If the process is defined in a domain \(\mathcal{D}\subset\mathbb{R}\) then it is necessary to introduce boundary conditions that specify the behavior at the edges of the definition set.
A typical example is the one in which \(\mathcal{D}=\mathbb{R}^+\), which we have already studied in section (sec:diffusione-in-un-dominio-semi-infinito) in the diffusion process. The possibilities, let’s remember, are two:


12.2.3 Equilibrium solution
For the equation to admit an equilibrium solution, it is necessary to assume that the functions \(\mu(x,t)\) and \(\sigma(x,t)\) do not explicitly depend on time, since the probability density \(p(x,t)\) explicitly depends on them.
By definition, a stationary solution of the Fokker-Planck equation is such that
\[\begin{equation}
    p^{st}(x)=\lim_{t\to\infty}p(x,t)
\end{equation}\]
It follows that the time derivative of \(p^{st}(x)\) is zero, and by substituting in the Fokker-Planck equation we obtain:
\[\begin{equation*}
    -\frac{d}{dx}(\mu p^{st})+\frac{1}{2}\frac{d^2}{dx^2}\left(\sigma^2p^{st}\right)=0
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s factor out \(-\frac{d}{dx}\):
\[\begin{equation}
    -\frac{d}{dx}\left[\mu p^{st}-\frac{1}{2}\frac{d}{dx}\left(\sigma^2 p^{st}\right)\right]=0
\end{equation}\]
The term in square brackets is none other than the flux, as it was defined in (eq:fokker-planck-flusso-definizione). The derivative written above tells us that this flux must be constant, but for the solution to be stationary, the flux must not only be constant, but it must be zero:
\[\begin{equation}
    J^{st}(x)=0 \ \iff \ \mu p^{st}-\frac{1}{2}\frac{d}{dx}\left(\sigma^2p^{st}\right)=0
\end{equation}\]
To solve the differential equation, let’s isolate the derivative:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{d}{dx}\left(\sigma^2p^{st}\right)=2\mu p^{st}
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s multiply and divide on the right by \(\sigma^2\) so that we can separate the variables:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{d}{dx}\left(\sigma^2p^{st}\right)=\frac{2\mu}{\sigma^2}\left(\sigma^2p^{st}\right) \ \Longrightarrow \ \int\frac{d\left(\sigma^2p^{st}\right)}{\sigma^2p^{st}}=2\int_{x_{min}}^x\frac{\mu(x')}{\sigma(x')^2}dx'
\end{equation*}\]
\[\begin{equation*}
    \ln\left(\sigma^2p^{st}\right)=2\int_{x_{min}}^x\frac{\mu(x')}{\sigma(x')^2}dx'+c
\end{equation*}\]
From which it follows:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \sigma(x)^2p^{st}(x)=ke^{2\int_{x_{min}}^x\frac{\mu(x')}{\sigma(x')^2}dx'} \ \Longrightarrow \ p^{st}(x)=\frac{k}{\sigma(x)^2}e^{2\int_{x_{min}}^x\frac{\mu(x')}{\sigma(x')^2}dx'}
\end{equation*}\]
where \(k=e^{c}\).
To find \(k\) we must impose the normalization of the probability density:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \int_{x_{min}}^{x_{max}}p^{st}(x)dx=k\int_{x_{min}}^{x_{max}}\frac{1}{\sigma(x)^2}e^{2\int_{x_{min}}^{x_{max}}\frac{\mu(x')}{\sigma(x')^2}dx'}=1
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s set
\[\begin{equation*}
    Z=\frac{1}{k}=\int_{x_{min}}^{x_{max}}\frac{1}{\sigma(x)^2}e^{2\int_{x_{min}}^{x_{max}}\frac{\mu(x')}{\sigma(x')^2}dx'}
\end{equation*}\]
so as to obtain the complete solution in the following form:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{p^{st}(x)=\frac{1}{Z\sigma(x)^2}e^{2\int_{x_{min}}^x\frac{\mu(x')}{\sigma(x')^2}dx'}}
\end{equation}\]
Let’s pay attention to the fact that an equilibrium solution does not necessarily exist: what we have shown is that, if it exists, it has the form shown in (eq:fokker-planck-soluzione-all-equilibrio-forma-finale). This solution, moreover, does not depend in any way on the initial conditions of the system; it is therefore an intrinsic property of the system, depending only on the functions \(\mu(x)\) and \(\sigma(x)\).

title: 12  Applications in classical mechanics
text:
13 Applications in classical mechanics
So far we have limited ourselves to treating stochastic processes in the context of ideal mathematical systems. Below we will see how the tools learned in this regard can also be applied in real physical systems, in which quantities measurable in the laboratory are involved.

title: 12  Applications in classical mechanics
text:
13.1 Particle in an extended medium
The first system we are going to study is that of a particle of mass \(m\) moving in an extended medium. This medium is composed of smaller particles than the one considered, but large enough that the effect due to collisions cannot be neglected, and they constitute a thermostatic bath, homogeneous and isotropic, in thermal equilibrium at a constant temperature \(T\).
The particle, which we will refer to as a mesoscopic particle, will be subject to two types of forces: an external force \(\vec{F}_{ext}\) and the combination of the forces that each individual particle of the medium exerts on it.
Combining the two terms, the dynamics of the motion will be described by the following formula:
\[\begin{equation*}
    m\frac{d^2\vec{x}}{dt^2}=\vec{F}_{ext}\left(\vec{x}(t)\right)+\sum_{i=1}^N\vec{F}\left(\vec{x}(t)-\vec{x}_i(t)\right)
\end{equation*}\]
assuming that \(N\) is the total number of particles in the medium.
In realistic physical systems, the number \(N\) is of the order of Avogadro’s number, therefore it is not possible to integrate the differential equation to obtain the equation of motion, but it is necessary to use an effective approach that simplifies the description of the overall dynamics. We divide the contribution exerted by the individual particles into two components: the first is an average contribution \(\vec{F}_m\), which has the effect of slowing down the particle due to collisions, and is what we commonly call viscous friction, proportional to the particle’s velocity; the second is instead a contribution of a statistical nature \(\vec{F}_n\), which we can identify as noise. Let’s focus on the latter.
In order for \(\vec{F}_n\) to be treated as a stochastic process, we need to make explicit three conditions that we must assume to be valid for the system in question:
If all this is valid, the term \(\vec{F}_n\) is nothing more than the Gaussian white noise \(\zeta(t)\), since the symmetry of the system guarantees that there is no preferential direction (\(\langle\zeta(t)\rangle=0\)) and there is also no correlation at different times (\(\langle\zeta(t)\zeta(t')\rangle=\delta(t-t')\)).
To summarize, the total forces acting are three: the external force \(\vec{F}_{ext}\), the average force \(\vec{F}_m\propto-\frac{d\vec{x}}{dt}\) and the noise \(\vec{F}_n\propto\zeta(t)\). Assuming for simplicity that there is no external force acting on the system, and considering a one-dimensional system, the equation of dynamics will be written as follows:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{m\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}=-\gamma\frac{dx}{dt}+\sigma\zeta(t)}
\end{equation}\]

13.1.1 Connection with the diffusion process
We immediately notice a parallelism between the system just described and the Brownian motion described starting from the Markov process. In fact, as we will see below, the two phenomena are not at all disconnected.
To study how the variance relative to the position of the mesoscopic particle changes, it is necessary to find the expression for the derivative of \(\langle x(t)^2\rangle\).
Let’s make the second temporal derivative of \(x^2\) explicit:
\[\begin{align*}
    \frac{d^2\left(x^2\right)}{dt^2}&=\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{d\left(x^2\right)}{dt}\right)=\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{d\left(x^2\right)}{dx}\frac{dx}{dt}\right)=\frac{d}{dt}\left(2x\frac{dx}{dt}\right)=\\
    &=2\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+2x\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}
\end{align*}\]
Isolating the last term:
\[\begin{equation*}
    x\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}=\frac{1}{2}\frac{d^2\left(x^2\right)}{dt^2}-\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^2
\end{equation*}\]
We will therefore have to multiply both members of (eq:meccanica-classica-mezzo-ampio-equazione-differenziale) by \(x\) and substitute what we have just found:
\[\begin{equation*}
    mx\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}=-\gamma x\frac{dx}{dt}+\sigma x\zeta(t) \ \Longrightarrow \ \frac{m}{2}\frac{d^2\left(x^2\right)}{dt^2}-m\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^2=-\gamma x\frac{dx}{dt}+\sigma x\zeta(t)
\end{equation*}\]
We have already seen above that \(\frac{d\left(x^2\right)}{dt}=2x\frac{dx}{dt}\), so we can substitute in the first term of the second member:
\[\begin{equation}
    \frac{m}{2}\frac{d^2\left(x^2\right)}{dt^2}-m\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^2=-\frac{\gamma}{2}\frac{d\left(x^2\right)}{dt}+\sigma x\zeta(t)
\end{equation}\]
At this point we can apply the average value to the entire equation:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{m}{2}\frac{d^2\left\langle x^2\right\rangle}{dt^2}-m\left\langle\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^2\right\rangle=-\frac{\gamma}{2}\frac{d\left\langle x^2\right\rangle}{dt}+\sigma\langle x\zeta(t)\rangle
\end{equation*}\]
Using the Itô convention:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \langle x\zeta(t)\rangle=\langle x\rangle\cancel{\langle\zeta(t)\rangle}=0
\end{equation*}\]
Ordering the remaining terms with respect to the order of the derivative, we obtain:
\[\begin{equation}
    \frac{m}{2}\frac{d^2\left\langle x^2\right\rangle}{dt^2}+\frac{\gamma}{2}\frac{d\left\langle x^2\right\rangle}{dt}-m\left\langle\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^2\right\rangle=0
\end{equation}\]
At this point we can substitute the equation given by the equipartition theorem of energy, which tells us that, in a thermodynamic system at equilibrium, each degree of freedom contributes to the average value of the kinetic energy of the system with a quantity equal to \(\frac{1}{2}k_BT\) for each mole of substance.
Assuming we are considering only one mole of fluid, it follows that
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{m}{\cancel{2}}\left\langle\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^2\right\rangle=\frac{1}{\cancel{2}}k_BT
\end{equation*}\]
being only one degree of freedom in a one-dimensional system.
Let’s go back to (eq:meccanica-classica-mezzo-ampio-calcolo-varianza-1):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{m}{2}\frac{d^2\left\langle x^2\right\rangle}{dt^2}+\frac{\gamma}{2}\frac{d\left\langle x^2\right\rangle}{dt}-k_BT=0
\end{equation*}\]
To solve this differential equation we define
\[\begin{equation*}
    y(t)=\frac{d\left\langle x^2\right\rangle}{dt}
\end{equation*}\]
In this way we obtain a first-order differential equation:
\[\begin{equation}
    \frac{m}{2}\frac{dy}{dt}+\frac{\gamma}{2}y-k_BT=0
\end{equation}\]
Let’s consider the associated homogeneous equation, which has as its solution
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{m}{\cancel{2}}\frac{dy}{dt}+\frac{\gamma}{\cancel{2}}y=0 \ \Longrightarrow \ \int\frac{dy}{y}=-\frac{\gamma}{m}\int dt \ \Longrightarrow \ y(t)=ke^{-\frac{\gamma}{m}t}
\end{equation*}\]
with \(k\) constant.
To calculate the overall solution we need to add a constant contribution \(c\) such that
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{m}{2}\cancel{\frac{dc}{dt}}+\frac{\gamma}{2}c-k_BT=0 \ \iff \ c=\frac{2k_BT}{\gamma}
\end{equation*}\]
The solution will therefore be
\[\begin{equation}
    y(t)=ke^{-\frac{\gamma}{m}t}+\frac{2k_BT}{\gamma}
\end{equation}\]
For a system with suspended particles, it is typically found that \(\frac{m}{\gamma}\sim10^{-8} \ s\), which is a very small time interval; considering times \(t\gg10^{-8} \ s\), in fact, it is possible to neglect the exponential contribution, allowing us to write:
\[\begin{equation*}
    y(t)\simeq\frac{2k_BT}{\gamma}
\end{equation*}\]
which, as a function of \(x(t)\), becomes
\[\begin{equation}
    \frac{d\left\langle x^2\right\rangle}{dt}=\frac{2k_BT}{\gamma}
\end{equation}\]
We have thus reached the initial objective. The variance, in fact, will be given by the integration of this equation:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\left\langle x(t)^2\right\rangle=\left\langle x_0^2\right\rangle+\frac{2k_BT}{\gamma}t}
\end{equation}\]
We thus find a quantity linearly proportional to time \(t\), exactly as written in (eq:diffusione-varianza) derived precisely in the context of the study of Brownian motion.


13.1.2 Einstein relation
From the link between the diffusion process and the stochastic Wiener process, one of the four results obtained by Albert Einstein in his Annus Mirabilis (1905) also emerges.
To be able to derive it, however, it is first necessary to redefine the diffusion coefficient \(D\) that we have used so far by multiplying by a factor of \(2\), thus passing from \(D\) to \(2D\). That said, it is sufficient to start from the stochastic differential equation that defines Brownian motion, written in (eq:processi-stocastici-prima-equazione-differenziale-stocastica):
\[\begin{equation}
    dx=\sqrt{2D}dB
\end{equation}\]
having set \(\mu=0\) since \(\vec{F}_{ext}\), and there is therefore no preferential direction.
Let’s integrate both members:
\[\begin{equation*}
    x(t)=x_0+\sqrt{2D}B(t)
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s square and take the average value of both members:
\[\begin{align*}
    \left\langle x(t)^2\right\rangle&=\left\langle x_0^2+2\sqrt{2D}x_0B(t)+2DB(t)^2\right\rangle=\\
    &=\left\langle x_0^2\right\rangle+2\sqrt{2D}\left\langle x_0\right\rangle\cancel{\left\langle B(t)\right\rangle}+2D\left\langle B(t)^2\right\rangle
\end{align*}\]
We thus obtain:
\[\begin{equation}
    \left\langle x(t)^2\right\rangle=\left\langle x_0^2\right\rangle+2Dt
\end{equation}\]
We can now equate the second members of (eq:meccanica-classica-mezzo-esteso-varianza-risultato-finale) and (eq:meccanica-classica-varianza-moto-browniano-aggiustata), finding the Einstein relation:
\[\begin{equation}
    \cancel{\left\langle x_0^2\right\rangle}+\frac{2k_BT}{\gamma}t=\cancel{\left\langle x_0^2\right\rangle}+2Dt \ \Longrightarrow \ \boxed{D=\frac{k_BT}{\gamma}}
\end{equation}\]
The reason why this equation is so important is because it reveals an intrinsic relationship between the microscopic properties of the system (the temperature \(T\) and the friction coefficient \(\gamma\)) and the macroscopic behavior of the particle (the diffusion coefficient \(D\)). It is also one of the first results of the fluctuation-dissipation theorem, according to which, in a thermodynamic system at equilibrium, the dissipative forces and the thermal fluctuations must balance each other: we note in fact that \(D\), which is constant, depends neither on the initial conditions nor on the nature of the interaction between the particles of the medium and the mesoscopic particle, and the ratio between \(T\) and \(\gamma\) therefore depends exclusively on the thermal equilibrium in which the system is located.
Furthermore, since \(D\) can be measured from (eq:meccanica-classica-varianza-moto-browniano-aggiustata), \(T\) is known and \(\gamma\) can be obtained from the relation \(\gamma=6\pi\eta r\), the Einstein relation made it possible to measure for the first time the Boltzmann constant \(k_B\), which in turn was useful for finding an estimate of Avogadro’s number, since the two quantities are linked by the relation \(k_B=\frac{R}{N_A}\) with \(R\) being the gas constant known since the previous century.

title: 12  Applications in classical mechanics
13  Birth and Death Processes
text:
14 Birth and Death Processes
The last category of stochastic processes that we will study are the birth and death processes, so called because they describe a multi-state system where states are progressively populated (a birth phenomenon) or abandoned (a death phenomenon).

title: 13  Birth and Death Processes
text:
14.1 Introduction
Let’s imagine numbering each state with an integer, as shown in figure (fig:processi-di-nascita-e-morte-schema). By convention, we consider the of birth towards states with increasing numbers, from which an inevitable of death towards states with decreasing numbers follows.

We hypothesize that in a time interval \(\Delta t\) an element of the system in a given state \(n\) has only three possibilities: to remain in state \(n\) or to move to one of the two neighboring states, be it \(n-1\) or \(n+1\).
Note that the system described in this way is to all intents and purposes equivalent to the random walk introduced in section (sec:derivazione-microscopica), with only two differences:
At this point, let’s introduce the birth rate \(b_n\geqslant0\) and the death rate \(d_n\geqslant0\) such that, in a sufficiently small time interval \(\Delta t\), we can define:
We have implicitly assumed here that the rates are constant over time, but in principle they could explicitly depend on time. Under this assumption, the process is called homogeneous.
We can now write the master equation that allows us to derive the propagator of the process. Considering state \(n\), at time \(t+\Delta t\) we have three possibilities:
Summing the three contributions:
\[\begin{equation*}
    p(n,t+\Delta t)=b_{n-1}\Delta tp(n-1,t)+d_{n+1}\Delta tp(n+1,t)+\left[1-\left(b_n+d_n\right)\Delta t\right]p(n,t)
\end{equation*}\]
Let’s move \(p(,t)\) to the first member and divide by \(\Delta t\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{p(n,t+\Delta t)-p(n,t)}{\Delta t}=b_{n-1}p(n-1,t)+d_{n+1}p(n+1,t)-\left(b_n+d_n\right)p(n,t)
\end{equation*}\]
In the limit for \(\Delta t\to0\):
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\frac{\partial}{\partial t}p(n,t)=b_{n-1}p(n-1,t)+d_{n+1}p(n+1,t)-\left(b_n+d_n\right)p(n,t)}
\end{equation}\]
By introducing the initial condition \(p(n,t_0)=\delta_{n,n_0}\), the birth and death process introduced here is also a Markov process, and we can prove it mathematically by verifying the Chapman-Kolmogorov equation ((eq:processi-stocastici-equazione-di-chapman-kolmogorov)), which in this context is written as:
\[\begin{equation*}
    p\left(n_2,t_2|n_0,t_0\right)=\sum_{n_1}p\left(n_2,t_2|n_1,t_1\right)p\left(n_1,t_1|n_0,t_0\right)
\end{equation*}\]
Applying the derivative to both members:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{\partial}{\partial t_2}p\left(n_2,t_2|n_0,t_0\right)=\frac{\partial}{\partial t_2}\sum_{n_1}p\left(n_2,t_2|n_1,t_1\right)p\left(n_1,t_1|n_0,t_0\right)
\end{equation*}\]
Since \(p\left(n_1,t_1|n_0,t_0\right)\) is independent of \(t_2\), it can be excluded from the derivation:
\[\begin{equation}
    \frac{\partial}{\partial t_2}p\left(n_2,t_2|n_0,t_0\right)=\sum_{n_1}\frac{\partial}{\partial t_2}p\left(n_2,t_2|n_1,t_1\right)p\left(n_1,t_1|n_0,t_0\right)
\end{equation}\]
Let’s calculate the derivative on the second member using (eq:processi-nascita-morte-propagatore):
\[\begin{align*}
    \frac{\partial}{\partial t_2}p\left(n_2,t_2|n_1,t_1\right)=&b_{n_2-1}p\left(n_2-1,t_2|n_1,t_1\right)+\\
    &+d_{n_2+1}p\left(n_2+1,t_2|n_1,t_1\right)-\\
    &-\left(b_{n_2}+d_{n_2}\right)p\left(n_2,t_2|n_1,t_1\right)
\end{align*}\]
Let’s substitute in (eq:processo-nascita-morte-chapman-kolmogorov-calcolo-1):
\[\begin{align*}
    \frac{\partial}{\partial t_2}p\left(n_2,t_2|n_0,t_0\right)=&b_{n_2-1}\sum_{n_1}p\left(n_2-1,t_2|n_1,t_1\right)p\left(n_1,t_1|n_0,t_0\right)+\\
    &+d_{n_2+1}\sum_{n_1}p\left(n_2+1,t_2|n_1,t_1\right)p\left(n_1,t_1|n_0,t_0\right)-\\
    &-\left(b_{n_2}+d_{n_2}\right)\sum_{n_1}p\left(n_2,t_2|n_1,t_1\right)p\left(n_1,t_1|n_0,t_0\right)
\end{align*}\]
Assuming the Chapman-Kolmogorov equation is valid and applying it to the three terms written above, we find:
\[\begin{align*}
    \frac{\partial}{\partial t_2}p\left(n_2,t_2|n_0,t_0\right)=&b_{n_2-1}p\left(n_2-1,t_2|n_0,t_0\right)+\\
    &+d_{n_2+1}p\left(n_2+1,t_2|n_0,t_0\right)-\\
    &-\left(b_{n_2}+d_{n_2}\right)p\left(n_2,t_2|n_0,t_0\right)
\end{align*}\]
which is precisely the propagator formula written in (eq:processi-nascita-morte-propagatore).

14.1.1 Boundary conditions
As we have done in the past, we can introduce two types of boundary conditions:
Keep in mind that the master equation written above (equation (eq:processi-nascita-morte-propagatore)) is valid only as long as a boundary state (whether reflecting or absorbing) is not , because in that case it is necessary to modify it appropriately.


14.1.2 Matrix formalism
It is possible to obtain a more compact structure by defining the vector
\[\begin{equation}
    \vec{p}(t)=
    \begin{pmatrix}
        p(0,t)\\
        p(1,t)\\
        p(2,t)\\
        \vdots
    \end{pmatrix}
\end{equation}\]
assuming that we have started numbering the states from \(0\) onwards.
To be able to write the master equation, we will therefore have to introduce a matrix \(W\) such that
\[\begin{equation}
    \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\vec{p}(n,t)=W\vec{p}(n,t)
\end{equation}\]
Each component \(p(n,t)\) will therefore be calculated as
\[\begin{equation}
    \frac{\partial}{\partial t}p(n,t)=\sum_{n'}W_{n,n'}p(n',t)
\end{equation}\]
To construct \(W\), let’s consider the \(n\)-th row, which, when multiplied by the vector \(\vec{p}(n,t)\), must return the second member of (eq:processi-nascita-morte-propagatore), therefore every element of the vector other than \(p(n-1,t)\), \(p(n,t)\) and \(p(n+1,t)\) must give a null contribution. It follows that each row of \(W\) contains all zeros except for three consecutive elements; using the Kronecker delta, we can indicate the element of the matrix in the following form:
\[\begin{equation}
    W_{n,n'}=b_{n-1}\delta_{n-1,n'}+d_{n+1}\delta_{n+1,n'}-(b_n+d_n)\delta_{n,n'}
\end{equation}\]
In essence, it is a tridiagonal matrix whose elements on the diagonal are all negative and those on the upper and lower diagonals are instead positive. Furthermore, for the overall flow to be conserved, the sum of the elements along each column must be zero. Intuitively, we can understand this by observing three consecutive rows of the matrix \(W\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    W=
    \begin{pmatrix}
        \ddots & \dots & \dots & \dots & \vdots\\
        \vdots & -\left(b_{n-1}+d_{n-1}\right) & d_n &  0 & \vdots\\
        \vdots & b_{n-1} & -\left(b_n+d_n\right) & d_{n+1} & \vdots\\
        \vdots & 0 & b_n & -\left(b_{n+1}+d_{n+1}\right) & \vdots\\
        \vdots & \dots & \dots & \dots & \ddots\\
    \end{pmatrix}
\end{equation*}\]
Formally, this is verified by imposing that \(\sum\limits_np(n,t)\) is constant:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\sum_np(n,t)=0 \ \iff \ \sum_n\sum_{n'}W_{n,n'}p(n',t)=0
\end{equation*}\]
The second member can be rewritten as
\[\begin{equation*}
    \sum_{n'}\left(\sum_nW_{n,n'}\right)p(n',t)=0
\end{equation*}\]
from which it follows that
\[\begin{equation*}
    \sum_nW_{n,n'}=0 \ \ \ \forall n'
\end{equation*}\]
Ultimately, the birth and death process can be rewritten in the following compact form:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\begin{cases}
        \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\vec{p}(t)=W\vec{p}\\
        \vec{p}(0)=\vec{p}_0
    \end{cases}
    \ \ \text{with} \ \
    \begin{cases}
        W_{n,n'}\geqslant0 \ \ &    ext{for} \ n\neq n'\\
        \sum\limits_nW_{n,n'}=0 &\forall n'
    \end{cases}}
\end{equation}\]}} \end{equation}
from which the solution follows
\[\begin{equation}
    \vec{p}(t)=\vec{p}_0e^{Wt}
\end{equation}\]

title: 13  Birth and Death Processes
text:
14.2 Process statistics

14.2.1 Expected value
For this calculation, we assume that the number of states is infinite and the index \(n\) ranges over the entire set \(\mathbb{Z}\).
By definition:
\[\begin{equation}
    \langle n\rangle=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}np(n.t)
\end{equation}\]
Let’s derive both members with respect to time:
\[\begin{equation}
    \frac{d}{dt}\langle n\rangle=\frac{d}{dt}\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}np(n.t)=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}n\frac{\partial}{\partial t}p(n.t)
\end{equation}\]
Let’s substitute the master equation (equation (eq:processi-nascita-morte-propagatore)):
\[\begin{align*}
    \frac{d}{dt}\langle n\rangle&=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}n\left[b_{n-1}p(n-1,t)+d_{n+1}p(n+1,t)-\left(b_n+d_n\right)p(n,t)\right]=\\
    &=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}nb_{n-1}p(n-1,t)+\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}nd_{n+1}p(n+1,t)-\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}n\left(b_n+d_n\right)p(n,t)
\end{align*}\]
We can change the indices of the first two summations so that both include the term \(p(n,t)\):
\[\begin{align*}
    \frac{d}{dt}\langle n\rangle&=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}(n+1)b_np(n,t)+\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}(n-1)d_np(n,t)-\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}n\left(b_n+d_n\right)p(n,t)=\\
    &=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}\left[\cancel{nb_n}+b_np(n,t)\cancel{+nd_n}-d_n\cancel{-nb_n}\cancel{-nd_n}\right]p(n,t)=\\
    &=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}b_np(n,t)-\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}d_np(n,t)
\end{align*}\]
We find:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\frac{d}{dt}\langle n\rangle=\left\langle b_n\right\rangle-\left\langle d_n\right\rangle}
\end{equation}\]
The result is intuitive: on the one hand we have \(\langle b_n\rangle\), which is the average net flow to the right, from which the average net flow to the left is subtracted; it is clear that the difference between the two will tell us how the average value of the system varies over time.


14.2.2 Variance
For the variance, we proceed in the same way:
\[\begin{align*}
   \frac{d}{dt}\left\langle n^2\right\rangle&=\frac{d}{dt}\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}n^2p(n,t)=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}n^2\frac{\partial}{\partial t}p(n,t)=\\
   &=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}n^2\left[b_{n-1}p(n-1,t)+d_{n+1}p(n+1,t)-\left(b_n+d_n\right)p(n,t)\right]=\\
   &=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}n^2b_{n-1}p(n-1,t)+\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}n^2d_{n+1}p(n+1,t)-\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}n^2\left(b_n+d_n\right)p(n,t)=\\
   &=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}(n+1)^2b_np(n,t)+\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}(n-1)^2d_np(n,t)-\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}n^2\left(b_n+d_n\right)p(n,t)=\\
   &=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}\left[\left(n^2+2n-1\right)b_n+\left(n^2-2n+1\right)d_n-n^2b_n-n^2d_n\right]p(n,t)=\\
   &=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}\left[\left(\cancel{n^2}+2n-1\cancel{-n^2}\right)b_n+\left(\cancel{n^2}-2n+1\cancel{-n^2}\right)d_n\right]p(n,t)=\\
   &=2\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}n\left(b_n-d_n\right)p(n,t)+\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}b_np(n,t)+\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}d_np(n,t)
\end{align*}\]
We find:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{\frac{d}{dt}\left\langle n^2\right\rangle=2\left\langle n\left(b_n-d_n\right)\right\rangle+\left\langle b_n\right\rangle+\left\langle d_n\right\rangle}
\end{equation}\]


14.2.3 Equilibrium distribution
Under the appropriate conditions, we can explicitly calculate the equilibrium distribution for a homogeneous birth and death process.
For simplicity, let’s impose a reflecting state at \(n=0\). At equilibrium, the total flow concerning any state \(n\) - both incoming and outgoing - is zero, therefore:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \frac{\partial}{\partial t}p(n,t)=0 \ \iff \ b_{n-1}p(n-1)+d_{n+1}p(n+1)-\left(b_n+d_n\right)p(n)=0
\end{equation*}\]
Rearranging the terms:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \green{b_{n-1}p(n-1)-d_np(n)}-\left[\orange{b_np(n)-d_{n+1}p(n+1)}\right]=0
\end{equation*}\]
The highlighted terms, individually, are nothing more than the net flow between one state and the next: in green, for example, we have the flow from state \(n-1\) to state \(n\) (positive) and that from state \(n\) to state \(n-1\) (negative); in orange, instead, it is the net flow between state \(n\) and state \(n+1\).
Indicating these flows with \(J\), we will therefore write:
\[\begin{equation*}
    J_{n-1}-J_n=0 \ \Longrightarrow \ J_n=J_{n-1}
\end{equation*}\]
Since this relationship holds between generic states, we can iterate the formula:
\[\begin{equation*}
    J_n=J_{n-1}=J_{n-2}=J_{n-3}=\dots
\end{equation*}\]
until we reach \(J_1\), beyond which there is only the state \(n=0\) and therefore the flow \(J_0\) relative to the exchange between states \(n=0\) and \(n=1\). Let’s remember that here we have set a reflecting boundary condition, so by construction \(J_0=0\), but consequently all the other flows will also be zero.
In general, therefore:
\[\begin{equation*}
    J_n=0
\end{equation*}\]
which translates to
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{b_np^s(n)=d_{n+1}p^s(n+1)}
\end{equation}\]
where the \(s\) indicates that the propagator is relative to the stationary condition, that is, at equilibrium.
The expression written above is called detailed balance and it is from this that we can calculate the propagator for any state \(n\) in stationary conditions. We will have in fact that:
\[\begin{equation*}
    b_{n-1}p^s(n-1)=d_np^s(n) \ \iff \ p^s(n)=\frac{b_{n-1}}{d_n}p^s(n-1)
\end{equation*}\]
Iterating the formula:
\[\begin{equation*}
    p^s(n,t)=\frac{b_{n-1}}{d_n}p^s(n-1)=\frac{b_{n-1}b_{n-2}}{d_nd_{n-1}}p^s(n-2)=\frac{b_{n-1}b_{n-2}b_{n-3}}{d_nd_{n-1}d_{n-2}}p^s(n-3)=\dots
\end{equation*}\]
We thus find
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{p^s(n)=\prod_{i=1}^n\frac{b_{i-1}}{d_i}p_0^s}
\end{equation}\]
having indicated \(p^s(0)\) with \(p_0^s\).
By calculating \(p_0^s\), therefore, we are able to find all the terms \(p(n)\) at equilibrium. It is obtained by imposing normalization:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \sum_{n=0}^\infty p^s(n)=1 \ \iff \ \sum_{n=0}^\infty\prod_{i=1}^n\frac{b_{i-1}}{d_i}p_0^s=1
\end{equation*}\]
Since \(p_0^s\) is constant:
\[\begin{equation*}
    p_0^s\sum_{n=0}^\infty\prod_{i=1}^n\frac{b_{i-1}}{d_i}=1
\end{equation*}\]
For \(n=0\) the product acts on an empty interval (since the index \(i\) should run from \(1\) to \(0\), so it is a non-existent set), therefore the result is the neutral term \(1\):
\[\begin{equation*}
    p_0^s\left(1+\sum_{n=1}^\infty\prod_{i=1}^n\frac{b_{i-1}}{d_i}\right)=1
\end{equation*}\]
From which we find the final formula:
\[\begin{equation}
    \boxed{p_0^s=\left(1+\sum_{n=1}^\infty\prod_{i=1}^n\frac{b_{i-1}}{d_i}\right)^{-1}}
\end{equation}\]

title: 13  Birth and Death Processes
section: 14.3 Examples of birth and death processes
text:
14.3 Examples of birth and death processes

14.3.1 Poisson process
The Poisson process is the simplest nontrivial example of a birth–death family: it is a pure-birth process obtained by taking a constant birth rate and zero death rate. Concretely, for all states \(n=0,1,2,\dots\) we set \[
b_n=\lambda>0,\qquad d_n=0.
\] Thus births occur at a constant rate \(\lambda\) while no deaths are possible. This choice simplifies the master equation and yields the closed-form, well-known Poisson law for the state probabilities.
Master equation. Substituting the rates above into the general master equation gives \[\begin{equation}
\dot p_n(t)=\lambda\bigl(p_{n-1}(t)-p_n(t)\bigr),\qquad p_n(0)=\delta_{n,0},
\end{equation}\] where by convention we set \(p_{-1}(t)\equiv0\) so the equation is valid for \(n\ge0\).
Expectation. To obtain the mean count, multiply \(\eqref{eq:poisson-master}\) by \(n\) and sum over \(n\ge0\). Using index shifts and the normalization \(\sum_{n\ge0}p_n(t)=1\) we find \[
\frac{d}{dt}\langle n(t)\rangle
=\sum_{n\ge0} n\dot p_n(t)
=\lambda\sum_{n\ge0} n\bigl(p_{n-1}-p_n\bigr)
=\lambda\sum_{n\ge0} p_n(t)
=\lambda.
\] Since \(\langle n(0)\rangle=0\), integration yields \[
\boxed{\langle n(t)\rangle=\lambda t.}
\] Generating function and explicit solution. A compact route to the full distribution uses the probability generating function \[
g(z,t)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty z^n p_n(t),\qquad |z|\le1.
\] Multiplying \(\eqref{eq:poisson-master}\) by \(z^n\) and summing over \(n\ge0\) (again taking \(p_{-1}\equiv0\)) produces the linear first-order PDE \[
\frac{\partial}{\partial t}g(z,t)=\lambda(z-1)g(z,t),
\qquad g(z,0)=1,\quad g(1,t)=1.
\] Solving this ordinary differential equation in \(t\) (for fixed \(z\)) gives \[
g(z,t)=\exp\bigl(\lambda(z-1)t\bigr).
\] Expanding the exponential in powers of \(z\) and matching coefficients yields the state probabilities \[
\boxed{p_n(t)=e^{-\lambda t}\frac{(\lambda t)^n}{n!},\qquad n=0,1,\dots}
\] Hence, at time \(t\) the count is Poisson-distributed with parameter \(\lambda t\).
Variance. Differentiating the generating function with respect to \(z\) and evaluating at \(z=1\) provides moments: \[
g_z(z,t)=\lambda t\,e^{\lambda(z-1)t},\qquad
g_{zz}(z,t)=(\lambda t)^2e^{\lambda(z-1)t}.
\] Therefore \(E[n]=g_z(1,t)=\lambda t\) and \(E[n(n-1)]=g_{zz}(1,t)=(\lambda t)^2\), so \[
\operatorname{Var}(n(t))=E[n^2]-E[n]^2=E[n(n-1)]+E[n]-E[n]^2=\lambda t.
\] Thus the variance grows linearly in time and equals the mean: \[
\boxed{\operatorname{Var}(n(t))=\lambda t.}
\] Remarks. Summarizing the principal properties and consequences:
A classic example of a birth–death process is radioactive decay. Initially the system contains \(N_0\) identical radioactive particles; each particle decays independently at constant rate \(\gamma>0\). At time \(t\) the system has \(n\) surviving particles; in a short interval \(\Delta t\) the probability that a single surviving particle decays is \(\gamma\Delta t\), hence the probability that one decay occurs (given \(n\) survivors) is \(\gamma n\Delta t\). Thus this is a pure-death process with \[
b_n=0,\qquad d_n=\gamma n.
\] Master equation. For \(n=0,1,\dots,N_0\) the forward equation reads \[\begin{equation}
\dot P_n(t)=\gamma (n+1)P_{n+1}(t)-\gamma n P_n(t),
\end{equation}\] with the convention \(P_{N_0+1}(t)\equiv0\) and initial condition \[
P_n(0)=\delta_{n,N_0}.
\] For \(n=N_0\) \(\eqref{eq:radioactive-me}\) reduces to \(\dot P_{N_0}=-\gamma N_0 P_{N_0}\).
Evolution of the mean. From \(\eqref{eq:radioactive-me}\) we obtain \[\begin{align*}
\frac{d}{dt}\langle n(t)\rangle
&=\sum_{n=0}^{N_0} n\dot P_n(t)
=\gamma\sum_{n=0}^{N_0} n\bigl((n+1)P_{n+1}-nP_n\bigr).
\end{align*}\] Change index in the first sum by \(m=n+1\): \[
\sum_{n=0}^{N_0} n(n+1)P_{n+1}=\sum_{m=1}^{N_0+1}(m-1)mP_m=\sum_{m=0}^{N_0} (m-1)mP_m,
\] (using \(P_{N_0+1}=0\)). Hence \[
\frac{d}{dt}\langle n\rangle
=\gamma\sum_{m=0}^{N_0}\bigl((m-1)m-m^2\bigr)P_m
=-\gamma\sum_{m=0}^{N_0} m P_m
=-\gamma\langle n\rangle.
\] Therefore \[
\boxed{\frac{d}{dt}\langle n(t)\rangle=-\gamma\langle n(t)\rangle,\qquad
\langle n(t)\rangle=N_0 e^{-\gamma t}.}
\] Generating function solution. Define the probability generating function \[
g(z,t)=\sum_{n=0}^{N_0} z^n P_n(t),\qquad g(z,0)=z^{N_0},\quad g(1,t)=1.
\] Multiplying \(\eqref{eq:radioactive-me}\) by \(z^n\) and summing yields the first-order PDE \[
\frac{\partial g}{\partial t}=\gamma(1-z)\frac{\partial g}{\partial z}.
\] Solving by characteristics (or by observing that \(h(z,t)=1+(z-1)e^{-\gamma t}\) is an invariant curve) gives \[
\boxed{g(z,t)=\bigl(1+(z-1)e^{-\gamma t}\bigr)^{N_0}
=\bigl(e^{-\gamma t}z+(1-e^{-\gamma t})\bigr)^{N_0}.}
\] Expanding by the binomial theorem and equating coefficients of \(z^n\) yields the closed-form probabilities \[
\boxed{P_n(t)=\binom{N_0}{n}\bigl(e^{-\gamma t}\bigr)^n\bigl(1-e^{-\gamma t}\bigr)^{\,N_0-n},\qquad n=0,\dots,N_0.}
\] Interpretation. Each particle survives to time \(t\) independently with probability \(e^{-\gamma t}\); therefore the number of survivors is Binomial\((N_0,e^{-\gamma t})\). From this one immediately recovers \[
\langle n(t)\rangle=N_0 e^{-\gamma t},\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(n(t))=N_0 e^{-\gamma t}(1-e^{-\gamma t}).
\] ## Furry process The Furry process (also called the Yule or linear birth process) models a multiplicative branching mechanism: each particle independently produces a new particle at rate \(\gamma\), so the total birth rate when there are \(n\) particles is proportional to \(n\). Physically, this can be used as a simple model for an electromagnetic cascade in an absorbing material: a high-energy electron enters the medium, undergoes bremsstrahlung and pair production, and a cascade of secondary particles is generated. Under the idealizing assumptions of independent splitting events and no losses other than branching, the process is a pure-birth process with \[
b_n=\gamma n,\qquad d_n=0,\qquad n\ge1,
\] and we consider a single initial particle so that \(p_n(0)=\delta_{n,1}\).
Master equation. For \(n\ge1\) the forward (master) equation is \[\begin{equation}
\dot p_n(t)=\gamma (n-1)p_{n-1}(t)-\gamma n p_n(t),
\qquad p_n(0)=\delta_{n,1}.
\end{equation}\]
Generating function solution. Introduce the probability generating function \[
G(z,t)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty z^n p_n(t),\qquad G(z,0)=z.
\] Multiplying \(\eqref{eq:yule-me}\) by \(z^n\) and summing over \(n\ge1\) yields the linear first-order PDE \[
\frac{\partial G}{\partial t}=\gamma z(z-1)\frac{\partial G}{\partial z},
\] which can be solved by the method of characteristics. The solution that satisfies \(G(z,0)=z\) is \[
G(z,t)=\frac{e^{-\gamma t}\,z}{1-(1-e^{-\gamma t})z}.
\] Expanding this geometric series produces the closed-form state probabilities \[\begin{equation}
p_n(t)=e^{-\gamma t}\bigl(1-e^{-\gamma t}\bigr)^{\,n-1},\qquad n\ge1,
\end{equation}\] which is a geometric distribution (supported on \(\{1,2,\dots\}\)) with success probability \(e^{-\gamma t}\).
Moments. Differentiating the generating function gives the mean and variance. One finds \[
\boxed{\mathbb{E}[N(t)]=e^{\gamma t},\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(N(t))=e^{\gamma t}\bigl(e^{\gamma t}-1\bigr).}
\] Remarks. The Furry/Yule process captures the exponential growth of particle number in an idealised branching cascade and yields a simple geometric law for the multiplicity at fixed time. Its assumptions (independent identical branching, no energy loss, no absorption except by branching) limit quantitative accuracy for real showers, but the model is useful for qualitative understanding and as a tractable stochastic example. ## The contact process
The contact process (a mean-field SIS-type epidemic on a finite population) models transmission and recovery on a population of fixed size \(N\). Individuals are either infected or susceptible; let \(n\in\{0,\dots,N\}\) denote the number of infected individuals and set \(x=n/N\).
In a short time interval \(\Delta t\) the elementary transitions are
For large \(N\) it is convenient to introduce the intensive variable \(x=n/N\). With the scaling \[
b_x \equiv b_n\Big|_{n= Nx}\approx N\beta\,x(1-x),\qquad \beta\equiv\frac{\tilde\beta}{N-1}\,,
\] and \(d_x\equiv d_n\big|_{n=Nx}=N\gamma x\), the typical step in \(x\) is \(\Delta x=1/N\), so \(x\) changes by \(O(1/N)\). This separation of scales (existence of a large typical size \(N\)) justifies a diffusion approximation (Kurtz / Kramers–Moyal expansion) in the limit \(N\gg1\).
Master equation for the discrete probabilities \(q_n(t)=\Pr\{n(t)=n\}\) can be written in the rescaled form (indexing by \(x=n/N\)): \[
\dot q_n(t)=b_{x-\frac{1}{N}}\,q_{n-1}+d_{x+\frac{1}{N}}\,q_{n+1}-\bigl(b_x+d_x\bigr)\,q_n.
\] Writing \(p(x,t)\approx N q_n(t)\) as the density on the mesh \(\Delta x=1/N\) and Taylor-expanding the terms in powers of \(1/N\) yields, to second order, \[
\partial_t p(x,t)=-\frac{1}{N}\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\bigl[(b_x-d_x)\,p(x,t)\bigr]
+\frac{1}{2N^{2}}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}\bigl[(b_x+d_x)\,p(x,t)\bigr]+o\!\left(\frac{1}{N^{2}}\right).
\] Substituting \(b_x=N\beta x(1-x)\) and \(d_x=N\gamma x\) and multiplying by \(N\) gives the Fokker–Planck equation in the diffusive scaling \[
\boxed{\;\partial_t p(x,t)=-\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\Bigl[(\beta x(1-x)-\gamma x)\,p\Bigr]
+\frac{1}{2N}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}\Bigl[(\beta x(1-x)+\gamma x)\,p\Bigr]\; }.
\] The corresponding Itô stochastic differential equation for the fraction \(x(t)\) is \[
\boxed{\;dx=\bigl(\beta x(1-x)-\gamma x\bigr)\,dt+\sqrt{\frac{\beta x(1-x)+\gamma x}{N}}\;dB_t\;},
\] so demographic fluctuations are of order \(O(N^{-1/2})\).
Deterministic limit and moment closure. Taking expectations in the SDE or directly from the master equation gives the exact identity \[
\frac{d}{dt}\rho(t)=\frac{d}{dt}\mathbb{E}[x(t)]=(\beta-\gamma)\rho(t)-\beta\,\mathbb{E}[x^2(t)],
\] where \(\rho(t)=\mathbb{E}[x(t)]\). Closing the moment hierarchy with the mean-field approximation \(\mathbb{E}[x^2]\approx\rho^2\) yields the logistic equation \[
\boxed{\;\dot\rho=(\beta-\gamma)\rho-\beta\rho^2\;}
\] with steady states \(\rho^*=0\) and \(\bar\rho=(\beta-\gamma)/\beta\) (the latter exists only when \(\beta>\gamma\)). Linear stability analysis gives the Jacobian \(J(\rho)=\beta-\gamma-2\beta\rho\), hence
At the critical point \(\beta=\gamma\) the deterministic decay is algebraic: \(\rho(t)\sim 1/t\).
Comments and limitations.

title: 13  Birth and Death Processes
text:
14.4 Gillespie’s algorithm
The Gillespie algorithm is an exact stochastic simulation method for Markov jump processes on discrete state spaces. We derive the waiting-time and event-type distributions for a birth–death process and then state the algorithm; finally we give the straightforward generalisation to arbitrary jump rates.
Starting point: the master equation for a birth–death process is \[\begin{equation}
\dot p_n(t)=b_{n-1}p_{n-1}(t)+d_{n+1}p_{n+1}(t)-\bigl(b_n+d_n\bigr)p_n(t),
\end{equation}\] where \(b_n\) and \(d_n\) are the birth and death rates in state \(n\).
Waiting time to the next event. Let \(q_0(\tau\mid n)\) be the probability that, starting in state \(n\) at time \(0\), no transition occurs in the interval \([0,\tau)\). For a small increment \(\Delta\tau\) we have \[
q_0(\tau+\Delta\tau\mid n)=q_0(\tau\mid n)\bigl(1-(b_n+d_n)\Delta\tau\bigr).
\] Passing to the limit \(\Delta\tau\to0\) yields the ODE \[
\frac{d}{d\tau}q_0(\tau\mid n)=-\bigl(b_n+d_n\bigr)q_0(\tau\mid n),
\] with solution \[
\boxed{\,q_0(\tau\mid n)=\exp\bigl(-(b_n+d_n)\tau\bigr)\,}.
\] Thus the waiting time is exponential with rate \(\lambda_n\equiv b_n+d_n\). The probability density for the time of the next event is \[
\boxed{\,f(\tau\mid n)=\lambda_n e^{-\lambda_n\tau}\,}.
\] Type of the next event. Given that an event occurs at time \(\tau\), the conditional probability that it is a birth (rather than a death) depends only on the relative rates: \[
\Pr(\text{birth}\mid\text{event},n)=\frac{b_n}{b_n+d_n},\qquad
\Pr(\text{death}\mid\text{event},n)=\frac{d_n}{b_n+d_n}.
\] The joint densities for a birth or death at time \(\tau\) are therefore \[
q_+(\tau\mid n)=b_n e^{-\lambda_n\tau},\qquad
q_-(\tau\mid n)=d_n e^{-\lambda_n\tau}.
\] Gillespie algorithm (birth–death case)
Remarks.
General Markov jump processes. For a general jump process with transition rates \(T(n'\mid n)\ge0\) (probability to jump from \(n\) to \(n'\) in small time \(\Delta t\) is \(T(n'\mid n)\Delta t\) for \(n'\neq n\)), define the total exit rate \[
\lambda_n=\sum_{n'\neq n}T(n'\mid n).
\] Then the waiting time is exponential with rate \(\lambda_n\), and the probability to jump to a particular target state \(n'\) is \[
\Pr(n\to n')=\frac{T(n'\mid n)}{\lambda_n}\qquad(n'\neq n).
\] The general Gillespie step is identical to the birth–death case but with the discrete selection of the target state performed according to the discrete distribution proportional to \(T(n'\mid n)\).
Master equation (general form). The forward equation for the probabilities is \[
\boxed{\,\dot p(n,t)=\sum_{m\neq n} \bigl[T(n\mid m)p(m,t)-T(m\mid n)p(n,t)\bigr]\,,}
\] which is equivalent to the birth–death master equation when only nearest-neighbour transitions are allowed.

title: 14  Ordinary differential equations
text:
15 Ordinary differential equations
In this chapter we will deal with ODEs (Ordinary Differential Equations) related to chemical reactions. Let’s clarify right away that by , in reality, we mean something more than what the term generally suggests: it includes all those systems composed of individuals of a certain type who react with individuals of the same type or of a different type, producing as a product individuals who may or may not be of the same nature.
\[\begin{equation*}
    \text{Reactant 1}   +   \text{Reactant 2}   \genfrac{}{}{-3pt}{}{\longrightarrow}{\longleftarrow}   \text{Products}
\end{equation*}\]
In the generic reaction written above, we notice the presence of two arrows that indicate both directions: the reason is that, in the course of the discussion, we will also consider reversible reactions, which can therefore allow the system to return to its original state.
The reason why we consider systems of this type is that they form a wide field of applications where both stochastic and deterministic models prove to be very useful. Some specific examples are the following:
The objective is to write ordinary differential equations for the concentrations of the reactants and products starting from the rules that we know govern these chemical reactions. Furthermore, we will only deal with the deterministic dynamics, thus ignoring the statistical ones.

title: 14  Ordinary differential equations
14  Ordinary differential equations
14  Ordinary differential equations
text:
15.6 Fluctuations

15.6.1 Deterministic limit
Molecules (or individuals) are discrete entities that collide at random; when the required colliding set meets, the reaction occurs at some rate. The discrete nature of reacting entities therefore generates random events and hence stochasticity in the dynamics. The deterministic mass–action ODEs derived earlier are recovered only in the limit of large system size (many molecules), when fluctuations become negligible. In what follows we write the chemical Master Equation (CME) for a general well‑mixed system, state the combinatorial propensities, and perform the standard system‑size (Kramers–Moyal) expansion that connects the CME to the deterministic mass‑action ODEs and to a mesoscopic diffusion approximation.
Let \(\vec{n}=(n_1,n_2,\dots,n_S)^\top\) be the vector of molecule counts for species \(X_1,\dots,X_S\). Consider \(R\) reactions. For reaction \(r\) denote the reactant and product stoichiometric coefficients by \(N_{ir}\) and \(M_{ir}\) respectively, and define the stoichiometric change vector \[
\vec{s}_r=(M_{1r}-N_{1r},\;M_{2r}-N_{2r},\;\dots,\;M_{Sr}-N_{Sr})^\top.
\]
If reaction \(r\) fires in the forward direction it maps \[
\vec{n}\xrightarrow{T_r^+(\vec{n})}\vec{n}+\vec{s}_r,
\] whereas a backward (reverse) firing maps \[
\vec{n}\xrightarrow{T_r^-(\vec{n})}\vec{n}-\vec{s}_r.
\]
For a well‑mixed system the combinatorial propensities (transition rates) take the form \[\begin{align}
T_r^+(\vec{n}) &= k_r^+ \prod_{i=1}^S \binom{n_i}{N_{ir}}, \\
T_r^-(\vec{n}) &= k_r^- \prod_{i=1}^S \binom{n_i}{M_{ir}},
\end{align}\] because for reaction \(r\) to occur one must choose, for each species \(i\), an unordered set of \(N_{ir}\) reactant molecules out of the \(n_i\) present. These \(T_r^\pm\) are also called propensities.
The chemical Master Equation for the probability \(p(\vec{n},t)\) of being in state \(\vec{n}\) at time \(t\) is \[
\partial_t p(\vec{n},t)=\sum_{r=1}^R\Big[\,T_r^+(\vec{n}-\vec{s}_r)\,p(\vec{n}-\vec{s}_r,t)-T_r^+(\vec{n})\,p(\vec{n},t)
+T_r^-(\vec{n}+\vec{s}_r)\,p(\vec{n}+\vec{s}_r,t)-T_r^-(\vec{n})\,p(\vec{n},t)\Big].
\] This CME is an exact description of the stochastic well‑mixed model and can be simulated exactly using Gillespie’s stochastic simulation algorithm.
To obtain the deterministic limit introduce a system‑size parameter \(\Omega\) (for example the physical reaction volume). Define concentrations \[
\vec{x}=\frac{\vec{n}}{\Omega},
\] and assume molecule counts scale with \(\Omega\) so that typical \(n_i=O(\Omega)\) and the net reaction fluxes are extensive. For large \(\Omega\) the combinatorial factors satisfy the leading‑order approximation \[
\binom{\Omega x_i}{N_{ir}}\simeq\frac{(\Omega x_i)^{N_{ir}}}{N_{ir}!}\qquad(\Omega\to\infty),
\] hence, with suitably scaled rate constants, \[
T_r^\pm(\Omega\vec{x})\simeq \Omega\,v_r^\pm(\vec{x}),
\] where the macroscopic per‑unit‑volume reaction rates are \[\begin{align}
v_r^+(\vec{x}) &= k_r^+\prod_{i=1}^S\frac{x_i^{N_{ir}}}{N_{ir}!},\\
v_r^-(\vec{x}) &= k_r^-\prod_{i=1}^S\frac{x_i^{M_{ir}}}{M_{ir}!},
\end{align}\] and the net macroscopic rate per unit volume is \(v_r(\vec{x})\equiv v_r^+(\vec{x})-v_r^-(\vec{x})\).
Multiplying the CME by \(\vec{n}\) and summing over all states gives the exact evolution for the mean counts: \[
\frac{d}{dt}\langle\vec{n}\rangle=\sum_{r=1}^R\vec{s}_r\Big\langle T_r^+(\vec{n})-T_r^-(\vec{n})\Big\rangle.
\] In concentrations this reads \[
\frac{d}{dt}\langle\vec{x}\rangle=\sum_{r=1}^R\vec{s}_r\left[\frac{\langle T_r^+(\Omega\vec{x})\rangle}{\Omega}-\frac{\langle T_r^-(\Omega\vec{x})\rangle}{\Omega}\right].
\]
The right‑hand side above must be \(O(1)\) as \(\Omega\to\infty\) for a nontrivial macroscopic limit. Under the scaling stated earlier one finds \[
\frac{\langle T_r^\pm(\Omega\vec{x})\rangle}{\Omega}\simeq v_r^\pm(\langle\vec{x}\rangle)+o(1),
\] provided fluctuations are negligible. If correlations and higher cumulants vanish in the large‑\(\Omega\) limit (mean‑field or moment‑closure approximation), \[
\Big\langle\prod_i x_i^{N_{ir}}\Big\rangle \approx \prod_i\langle x_i\rangle^{N_{ir}},
\] and we recover the deterministic mass–action ODEs (the law of large numbers limit): \[
\frac{d}{dt}x_i=\sum_{r=1}^R s_{ir}\,v_r(\vec{x}),\qquad i=1,\dots,S.
\] These are the same mass–action ODEs written earlier. The approximation is valid when molecule numbers are large, the system is well mixed, and correlations are negligible.
A more refined approximation is obtained by the Kramers–Moyal (system‑size) expansion of the CME to second order. This yields a chemical Fokker–Planck equation (diffusive approximation) for the probability density of \(\vec{x}\): \[
\partial_t p(\vec{x},t)=-\sum_{i=1}^S\frac{\partial}{\partial x_i}\big[A_i(\vec{x})\,p\big]+\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j=1}^S\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x_i\partial x_j}\big[B_{ij}(\vec{x})\,p\big],
\] with drift and diffusion coefficients \[\begin{align}
A_i(\vec{x}) &= \sum_{r=1}^R s_{ir}\,v_r(\vec{x}), \\
B_{ij}(\vec{x}) &= \frac{1}{\Omega}\sum_{r=1}^R s_{ir}s_{jr}\,\big(v_r^+(\vec{x})+v_r^-(\vec{x})\big).
\end{align}\] The drift equals the deterministic mass‑action velocity and the diffusion is of order \(1/\Omega\).
The Fokker–Planck equation corresponds (not uniquely) to the Chemical Langevin Equation (CLE), which provides a mesoscopic stochastic differential equation approximation: \[
dx_i=\sum_{r=1}^R s_{ir}\,v_r(\vec{x})\,dt+\frac{1}{\sqrt{\Omega}}\sum_{r=1}^R s_{ir}\sqrt{v_r^+(\vec{x})+v_r^-(\vec{x})}\;dW_r(t),
\] where the \(dW_r\) are independent standard Wiener increments (one noise per reaction channel). The CLE shows that fluctuations around the deterministic trajectory scale as \(\Omega^{-1/2}\).
Remarks and conditions of validity:
In summary, starting from the discrete stochastic description (CME) with combinatorial propensities one recovers the deterministic mass‑action ODEs by (i) introducing concentrations, (ii) imposing the appropriate scaling of rate constants with system size, and (iii) applying a mean‑field closure. The leading stochastic corrections are captured by the Fokker–Planck equation and the CLE, which quantify fluctuations of order \(\Omega^{-1/2}\) about the deterministic trajectory.

title: 15  14-Exercises
16  Pattern di Turing
text:
17 Pattern di Turing
Se alle reazioni chimiche studiate nel precedente capitolo aggiungiamo anche la possibilità che gli elementi (reagenti e prodotti) possano muoversi seguendo processi di diffusione, viene a presentarsi la possibilità della formazione di strutture eterogenee dalla forma caratteristica, come osserviamo in natura nei molluschi, nei fiori, nelle farfalle e così via: si tratta dei pattern di Turing, così chiamati perché introdotti per la prima volta dal matematico Alan Turing in un suo lavoro pubblicato nel 1952 chiamato The Chemical Basis of Morphogenesis.
Ribadiamo anche in questo caso che per non intendiamo necessariamente reazioni aventi per soggetti molecole o speci propriamente chimiche, ma qualunque cosa che possa in qualche modo reagire e produrre un elemento (di qualsiasi natura esso sia) diverso.
Una caratteristica importante dei pattern di Turing è che si tratta di strutture emergenti, nel senso che non vi è alcuna informazione nel mondo microscopico relativo agli elementi e alle reazioni che è esplicitamente riferita alle strutture macroscopiche che si vengono a formare. In altre parole, è il gioco che si instaura tra processi di reazione e processi di diffusione a permettere la formazione di pattern su larga scala, i quali non possiedono in sé alcuna proprietà relativa alle strutture che emergono a scale più grandi.

title: 16  Pattern di Turing
16  Pattern di Turing
